# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Controlled Rectifier?

A **Controlled Rectifier** is a power electronic converter that converts fixed AC supply voltage into a variable DC output voltage. The control over the DC output is achieved by varying the **firing angle (α)** of the thyristors (SCRs). Unlike an uncontrolled rectifier (using only diodes), the onset of conduction in a thyristor-based rectifier is delayed by an angle α, which directly controls the average DC output voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A controlled rectifier is the building block of all DC motor drives, battery chargers, HVDC transmission systems, and electro-chemical processes. The firing angle **α** is measured from the **natural commutation point** (the instant a diode would have started conducting).

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Topologies of This Module

| Topology | Devices | Load | Conduction Mode |
|----------|---------|------|-----------------|
| 1-φ Half Controlled (Semi-converter) | 2 SCRs + 2 Diodes | R | Discontinuous |
| 1-φ Fully Controlled (Full-converter) | 4 SCRs | RL (Continuous) | Continuous |
| 3-φ Half Wave Controlled | 3 SCRs | RL (Continuous) | Continuous |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tap" Model

Imagine a water tap connected to a high-pressure pipe. A diode is like a tap that opens *automatically* the moment pressure arrives. A thyristor, however, is like a tap that needs an **external trigger (gate pulse)** to open. Once opened, it stays open until the water pressure reverses (AC crosses zero). By *delaying the trigger*, you control **how much water** flows into the tank per cycle — this delay is the **firing angle α**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used:**
> - **V_m = √2 · V_rms** — Peak value of AC supply voltage.
> - **ω = 2πf** — Angular frequency, where **f = 50 Hz** in India.
> - **V_avg** — Average (DC) output voltage over one complete cycle.

---

### 1.4 Why "Half Controlled" vs "Fully Controlled"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - A **Fully Controlled** bridge uses **4 thyristors** — the output voltage can be made *positive OR negative* (by pushing α beyond 90°), enabling **inversion (regenerative braking)**.
> - A **Half Controlled** bridge (semi-converter) uses **2 thyristors + 2 diodes** — the output voltage is always **positive (unipolar)**, simpler, cheaper, but **no inversion capability**.

This distinction is critical for KTU problems involving motor drives (which need regeneration) versus battery chargers (which do not).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Single Phase Half Controlled Rectifier (Semi-Converter) with R-Load

### 2.1.1 Circuit Topology
The semi-converter bridge consists of:
- **T1, T3** — Thyristors in the upper half (positive group).
- **D2, D4** — Diodes in the lower half (negative group).
- AC source Vs = Vm sin(ωt) and load resistor **R**.

### 2.1.2 Modes of Operation (R-Load)

**Mode 1: α ≤ ωt ≤ π — Conduction through T1 and D2**
- At ωt = α, T1 is fired. Current flows: Source(+ve) → T1 → R → D2 → Source(−ve).
- Output voltage follows the supply: **V₀ = Vm sin(ωt)**.

**Mode 2: π ≤ ωt ≤ (π + α) — Freewheeling through D2 and D4**
- At ωt = π, the supply reverses. T1 cannot conduct reverse current, so it turns OFF.
- D2 is already conducting; the load current (which was flowing through D2) is forced to commutate to D4. Now **D2 and D4** conduct simultaneously, **short-circuiting the load**.
- Output voltage: **V₀ = 0** (freewheeling action).

**Mode 3: (π + α) ≤ ωt ≤ 2π — T3 and D4 conduct**
- T3 is fired at ωt = (π + α). Output: **V₀ = |Vm sin(ωt)|** (negative half is rectified).

### 2.1.3 Average Output Voltage Derivation

The output is periodic with period π (half-wave symmetry in absolute value, shifted).

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

Integrating:

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \big( 1 + \cos \alpha \big)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Formula (Semi-Converter, R-Load):**
> $$V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big(1 + \cos \alpha\big) = \frac{\sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}}{\pi}\big(1 + \cos \alpha\big)$$

---

## 2.2 Single Phase Fully Controlled Bridge Rectifier (Continuous Conduction, RL Load)

### 2.2.1 Circuit Topology
- **T1, T2, T3, T4** — Four thyristors in H-bridge configuration.
- Load: **R-L** with large L such that load current is **continuous and ripple-free** (idealized as a constant current source I₀).

### 2.2.2 Conduction Sequence

| Interval | Conducting Pair | Output Voltage |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| α → (π + α) | T1, T2 | V₀ = +Vm sin(ωt) |
| (π + α) → (2π + α) | T3, T4 | V₀ = +Vm sin(ωt) |

> [!NOTE]
> T1–T2 are fired at α; T3–T4 are fired at (π + α). The output voltage waveform has a **period of π**, but is **shifted by α** from the supply.

### 2.2.3 Average Output Voltage

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \big( \cos \alpha - \cos(\pi + \alpha) \big)
$$

Using cos(π + α) = −cos α:

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2\cos \alpha = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula (Full-Converter, Continuous Conduction):**
> $$V_{0,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha = \frac{2 \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}}{\pi} \cos \alpha$$

> [!WARNING]
> This formula is **ONLY valid for continuous conduction mode (CCM)**. If the load inductance is insufficient, the current becomes discontinuous and the formula must be modified.

### 2.2.4 RMS Output Voltage

$$
V_{0,rms} = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) } = V_m \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\pi}\left[ \frac{\pi}{2} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2} \right] }
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> $$V_{0,rms} = V_m \sqrt{ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi} }$$

---

## 2.3 Three Phase Half Wave Controlled Rectifier (3-φ, 3-Pulse Converter)

### 2.3.1 Circuit Topology
- **T1, T2, T3** — Three thyristors, each connected between a phase (R, Y, B) and the **common load return (Neutral)**.
- The cathodes are joined to the **positive terminal** of the load (since this is a **P-side converter** supplying positive DC).
- Load: **R-L** with continuous, constant current I₀.

### 2.3.2 Phase Voltages
$$
V_{RN} = V_m \sin(\omega t), \quad V_{YN} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 120°), \quad V_{BN} = V_m \sin(\omega t - 240°)
$$

### 2.3.3 Natural Commutation Points
A diode would conduct when its phase voltage becomes the **highest** among the three. The crossover points occur at:
- **ωt = 30°** (R takes over from B)
- **ωt = 150°** (Y takes over from R)
- **ωt = 270°** (B takes over from Y)

These are separated by **120°**. With thyristors, conduction is **delayed by α** from these natural commutation points.

### 2.3.4 Conduction Sequence

| Interval | Conducting SCR | Output Voltage |
|----------|----------------|----------------|
| (30° + α) → (150° + α) | T1 | V₀ = V_RN |
| (150° + α) → (270° + α) | T2 | V₀ = V_YN |
| (270° + α) → (30° + α + 360°) | T3 | V₀ = V_BN |

### 2.3.5 Average Output Voltage

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{1}{2\pi/3} \int_{30° + \alpha}^{150° + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

Integrating:

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{30° + \alpha}^{150° + \alpha}
$$

$$
= \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ \cos(30° + \alpha) - \cos(150° + \alpha) \Big]
$$

Using the identity cos(A) − cos(B) = 2 sin((A+B)/2) sin((B−A)/2):

- A = 30° + α, B = 150° + α
- (A+B)/2 = 90° + α
- (B−A)/2 = 60°

$$
\cos(30° + \alpha) - \cos(150° + \alpha) = 2 \sin(90° + \alpha)\sin(60°) = 2\cos(\alpha) \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \sqrt{3}\cos\alpha
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Formula (3-φ Half-Wave, Continuous Conduction):**
> $$V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{6}\,V_{rms,L-N}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{2}\,V_{rms,L-L}}{2\pi}\cos\alpha$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas assume ideal thyristors, ideal sources, and continuous conduction where applicable.**

| # | Topology | Average V₀ | RMS V₀ | Range of α |
|---|----------|-----------|--------|------------|
| 1 | 1-φ Semi-converter, R-Load | (Vm / π)(1 + cos α) | (Vm / √2) √[1 − (α/π) − (sin 2α / 2π)] | 0° ≤ α ≤ 180° |
| 2 | 1-φ Full-converter, RL (CCM) | (2 Vm / π) cos α | Vm · √[1/2 + (sin 2α)/(2π)] | 0° ≤ α ≤ 180° |
| 3 | 3-φ Half-Wave, RL (CCM) | (3√3 Vm / 2π) cos α | (Vm) · √[1/2 + (3√3 cos 2α)/(8π)] | 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° (rectification) |

**Where:**
- $V_m = \sqrt{2}\,V_{rms}$ (peak phase voltage for 1-φ and 3-φ half-wave)
- $V_{rms,L-L} = \sqrt{3}\,V_{rms,L-N}$ for the 3-φ case
- $I_{0,avg} = V_{0,avg} / R$ for purely resistive loads
- $I_{0,avg} = I_0$ (assumed constant) for highly inductive loads

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention:** For α < 90°, V₀ > 0 → **Rectifier Mode**. For α > 90°, V₀ < 0 → **Inverter Mode** (only possible with fully-controlled topologies).

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Semi-converter (R-Load):** DC welding sets, battery charging, low-power DC supplies.
- **Full-converter (RL-Load):** DC motor drives (separately excited), four-quadrant operation.
- **3-φ Half-Wave:** Legacy industrial DC drives, electroplating. (Largely replaced by 3-φ full-bridge in modern systems, but remains a KTU staple.)

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation 1: 1-φ Full-Converter Average Voltage (Verification)

We will re-derive the master formula by evaluating the integral with **every intermediate step** shown.

**Step 1: Identify the integration window.**
For a 1-φ full-bridge in continuous conduction, T1–T2 conduct from α to (π + α). Hence:

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 2: Pull out the constants.**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha} \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

**Step 3: Apply the antiderivative of sin(ωt).**

$$
\int \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = -\cos(\omega t) + C
$$

Therefore:

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\alpha}^{\pi + \alpha}
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the upper and lower limits.**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \left[ -\cos(\pi + \alpha) - \big( -\cos(\alpha) \big) \right] = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \big[ \cos \alpha - \cos(\pi + \alpha) \big]
$$

**Step 5: Apply the identity cos(π + α) = −cos(α).**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \big[ \cos \alpha - (-\cos \alpha) \big] = \frac{V_m}{\pi} \cdot 2 \cos \alpha
$$

**Step 6: Final result.**

$$
\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi} \cos \alpha}
$$

**Numerical sanity check (α = 0°):** V₀ = 2Vm/π ≈ 0.6366 Vm. For Vm = 325.27 V (230 V rms), V₀ ≈ 207.1 V. This matches the well-known value of a **full-wave bridge** at zero firing delay. ✓

**Numerical sanity check (α = 90°):** V₀ = 0. (Symmetry of +ve and −ve half cycles.) ✓

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation 2: 3-φ Half-Wave Average Voltage

**Step 1: Write the conduction window for T1.**

T1 conducts from ωt = (π/6 + α) to ωt = (5π/6 + α) (i.e., 30° + α to 150° + α).

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3}{2\pi} \int_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{5\pi/6 + \alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t) \, d(\omega t)
$$

(The factor 3/(2π) arises because the period is 2π/3 for one thyristor conduction.)

**Step 2: Integrate.**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(\omega t) \Big]_{\pi/6 + \alpha}^{5\pi/6 + \alpha}
$$

**Step 3: Apply limits.**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ -\cos(5\pi/6 + \alpha) + \cos(\pi/6 + \alpha) \Big] = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \Big[ \cos(\pi/6 + \alpha) - \cos(5\pi/6 + \alpha) \Big]
$$

**Step 4: Use cos(A) − cos(B) = 2 sin((A+B)/2) sin((B−A)/2).**

Let A = π/6 + α and B = 5π/6 + α:
- (A + B)/2 = (π/6 + 5π/6 + 2α)/2 = π/2 + α
- (B − A)/2 = (5π/6 − π/6)/2 = 4π/12 = π/3

Therefore:

$$
\cos(\pi/6 + \alpha) - \cos(5\pi/6 + \alpha) = 2 \sin(\pi/2 + \alpha)\sin(\pi/3) = 2\cos(\alpha) \cdot \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} = \sqrt{3}\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 5: Substitute back.**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3 V_m}{2\pi} \cdot \sqrt{3}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

**Step 6: Final boxed result.**

$$
\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha \approx 0.827\,V_m \cos\alpha}
$$

**Numerical check (α = 0°):** V₀ = (3√3 · Vm) / (2π) = 0.827 Vm. For a 400 V rms line-to-line 3-φ system, Vm(L-N) = 400·√2/√3 ≈ 326.6 V, giving V₀ ≈ 270.2 V. ✓

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 1 — 1-φ Full-Converter (Continuous Conduction)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Model Problem, Module 2]**
> A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier is supplied from **230 V, 50 Hz** AC source. The load is highly inductive with **R = 10 Ω** and the current is continuous and constant at **I₀ = 10 A**. The firing angle is **α = 30°**. Determine:
> (a) Average output voltage.
> (b) RMS output voltage.
> (c) Power delivered to the load.

**Given:**
- V_rms = 230 V → Vm = √2 × 230 = **325.27 V**
- f = 50 Hz → ω = 314.16 rad/s
- R = 10 Ω, I₀ = 10 A
- α = 30°

**Solution (a): Average output voltage.**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{2 \times 325.27}{\pi} \cos 30°
$$

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{650.54}{3.1416} \times 0.8660 = 207.07 \times 0.8660 \approx 179.32 \, \text{V}
$$

**Solution (b): RMS output voltage.**

$$
V_{0,rms} = V_m \sqrt{ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi} }
$$

sin(2 × 30°) = sin(60°) = 0.8660

$$
V_{0,rms} = 325.27 \sqrt{ \frac{1}{2} + \frac{0.8660}{2\pi} } = 325.27 \sqrt{ 0.5 + 0.1378 } = 325.27 \sqrt{0.6378}
$$

$$
V_{0,rms} = 325.27 \times 0.7986 \approx 259.78 \, \text{V}
$$

**Solution (c): Power delivered.**

For a constant load current I₀:

$$
P = I_0^2 \cdot R = (10)^2 \times 10 = 1000 \, \text{W} = 1 \, \text{kW}
$$

(Alternative: P = V₀,rms × I₀ = 259.78 × 10 ≈ 2597.8 W — this is the apparent power; the real power is I₀²R since the current is purely DC.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often use P = V₀,avg × I₀. This is **wrong** for computing **real power** with non-sinusoidal waveforms. Real power in this case is I₀² R because the current is constant (DC) and the only dissipating element is R.

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem 2 — 1-φ Semi-Converter (R-Load)

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Model Problem, Module 2]**
> A single-phase semi-converter feeds a resistive load of **R = 20 Ω** from a **230 V, 50 Hz** supply. Firing angle is **α = 60°**. Calculate:
> (a) Average output voltage.
> (b) RMS load current.
> (c) Average load current.

**Given:**
- Vm = 325.27 V
- R = 20 Ω, α = 60°
- cos 60° = 0.5, sin 120° = 0.866

**Solution (a):**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}(1 + \cos\alpha) = \frac{325.27}{\pi}(1 + 0.5) = 103.54 \times 1.5 \approx 155.31 \, \text{V}
$$

**Solution (b): RMS Load Current.**

For the semi-converter with R-load, the output voltage is Vm sin(ωt) from α to π, and 0 from π to (π + α). The RMS value:

$$
V_{0,rms} = V_m \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\pi}\left[ \frac{\pi - \alpha}{2} + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{4} \right] }
$$

Wait — let me re-derive cleanly:

$$
V_{0,rms}^2 = \frac{1}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} V_m^2 \sin^2(\omega t) \, d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m^2}{\pi} \int_{\alpha}^{\pi} \frac{1 - \cos(2\omega t)}{2} d(\omega t)
$$

$$
= \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ (\pi - \alpha) - \frac{\sin 2\pi - \sin 2\alpha}{2} \right] = \frac{V_m^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \alpha + \frac{\sin 2\alpha}{2} \right]
$$

For α = 60° = π/3, sin 120° = √3/2 ≈ 0.8660:

$$
V_{0,rms}^2 = \frac{(325.27)^2}{2\pi} \left[ \pi - \frac{\pi}{3} + \frac{0.8660}{2} \right] = \frac{105800.6}{6.2832} \left[ \frac{2\pi}{3} + 0.433 \right]
$$

$$
= 16840.0 \times (2.0944 + 0.4330) = 16840.0 \times 2.5274 = 42561.6
$$

$$
V_{0,rms} = \sqrt{42561.6} \approx 206.30 \, \text{V}
$$

$$
I_{0,rms} = \frac{V_{0,rms}}{R} = \frac{206.30}{20} \approx 10.31 \, \text{A}
$$

**Solution (c):**

$$
I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg}}{R} = \frac{155.31}{20} \approx 7.77 \, \text{A}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Problem 3 — 3-φ Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier

> [!NOTE]
> **[KTU University Exam - Model Problem, Module 2]**
> A 3-phase, 3-pulse (half-wave) controlled rectifier is fed from a **400 V, 50 Hz** line-to-line RMS, 3-phase source. The load is highly inductive with **R = 5 Ω**. The firing angle is **α = 30°**. Compute:
> (a) Average output voltage.
> (b) Average output current.
> (c) The new firing angle if V₀ is to be reduced to 50% of the value at α = 0°.

**Given:**
- V_LL,rms = 400 V → V_LN,rms = 400 / √3 = **230.94 V**
- Vm (L-N) = √2 × 230.94 = **326.60 V**
- R = 5 Ω, α = 30°

**Solution (a):**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 326.60}{2\pi} \cos 30°
$$

$$
= \frac{3 \times 1.732 \times 326.60}{6.2832} \times 0.8660 = \frac{1697.12}{6.2832} \times 0.8660
$$

$$
= 270.13 \times 0.8660 \approx 233.93 \, \text{V}
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg}}{R} = \frac{233.93}{5} \approx 46.79 \, \text{A}
$$

**Solution (c):**

At α = 0°, V₀,max = (3√3 Vm) / (2π) = 270.13 V.
We need V₀,new = 0.5 × 270.13 = 135.07 V.

$$
135.07 = 270.13 \cos(\alpha_{new}) \implies \cos(\alpha_{new}) = 0.5 \implies \alpha_{new} = 60°
$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | 1-φ Semi (R) | 1-φ Full (RL-CCM) | 3-φ Half-Wave (RL-CCM) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Max V₀ (α = 0°) | 2Vm/π ≈ 0.637 Vm | 2Vm/π ≈ 0.637 Vm | 3√3 Vm/2π ≈ 0.827 Vm |
| Min V₀ | 0 (at α = 180°) | −2Vm/π (at α = 180°) | 0 (at α = 90°) |
| Inversion Possible? | No (V₀ ≥ 0) | Yes (α > 90°) | No (for rectifier) |
| Rectification Range | 0° ≤ α ≤ 180° | 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° | 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° |
| Inversion Range | N/A | 90° < α ≤ 180° | N/A |
| Power Factor | Poor at high α | Poor at high α | Better (multi-pulse) |
| Ripple Frequency | 2f = 100 Hz | 2f = 100 Hz | 3f = 150 Hz |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Higher pulse number (3-φ) yields:
> 1. Higher average output voltage per unit Vm.
> 2. Lower ripple (3rd harmonic dominant vs 2nd for 1-φ).
> 3. Better transformer utilization.

```mermaid
graph TD

    GATE[GATE PULSE GENERATOR FIRING ANGLE ALPHA] --> BRIDGE
```

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[START AT OMEGA T EQUALS ALPHA] --> B[T1 T2 CONDUCT]
    B --> C[OUTPUT V0 EQUALS VM SIN OMEGA T]
    C --> D[OMEGA T EQUALS PI PLUS ALPHA]
    D --> E[T3 T4 CONDUCT]
    E --> F[OUTPUT V0 EQUALS VM SIN OMEGA T]
    F --> G[CYCLE COMPLETE PERIOD PI]
    G --> A
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture — 3-Phase Half Wave Controlled Rectifier

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC3P[3 PHASE AC SOURCE R Y B] --> T1A
    SRC3P --> T2A
    SRC3P --> T3A
    T1A[THYRISTOR T1] --> JOIN[COMMON DC POSITIVE BUS]
    T2A[THYRISTOR T2] --> JOIN
    T3A[THYRISTOR T3] --> JOIN
    JOIN --> LOAD3P[RL LOAD CONTINUOUS I0]
    LOAD3P --> NEUTRAL[NEUTRAL RETURN]
    GATE3P[SEQUENTIAL GATE PULSES DELAY ALPHA FROM 30 DEG 150 DEG 270 DEG] --> T1A
    GATE3P --> T2A
    GATE3P --> T3A
```

## 4.4 Conduction Timing Diagram — 3-Phase Half Wave

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[R PHASE T1 FIRES AT 30 PLUS ALPHA] --> Q1[CONDUCTS TILL 150 PLUS ALPHA]
    Q1 --> P2[Y PHASE T2 FIRES AT 150 PLUS ALPHA]
    P2 --> Q2[CONDUCTS TILL 270 PLUS ALPHA]
    Q2 --> P3[B PHASE T3 FIRES AT 270 PLUS ALPHA]
    P3 --> Q3[CONDUCTS TILL 390 PLUS ALPHA EQUALS 30 PLUS 360]
    Q3 --> P1
```

## 4.5 Semi-Converter (R-Load) Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    S1[ALPHA TO PI T1 D2 CONDUCT V0 EQUALS VM SIN OMEGA T] --> S2[PI TO PI PLUS ALPHA FREEWHEELING D2 D4 V0 EQUALS ZERO]
    S2 --> S3[PI PLUS ALPHA TO 2 PI T3 D4 CONDUCT V0 EQUALS VM SIN OMEGA T]
    S3 --> S4[CYCLE REPEATS PERIOD PI]
    S4 --> S1
```

## 4.6 Comparison Block Diagram — All Three Topologies

```mermaid
graph TD
    TOPO[CONTROLLED RECTIFIER TOPOLOGIES] --> A1[1 PHASE SEMI CONVERTER 2 SCR PLUS 2 DIODE R LOAD]
    TOPO --> A2[1 PHASE FULL CONVERTER 4 SCR RL LOAD CCM]
    TOPO --> A3[3 PHASE HALF WAVE 3 SCR RL LOAD CCM]
    A1 --> F1[V0 AVG EQUALS VM OVER PI TIMES 1 PLUS COS ALPHA]
    A2 --> F2[V0 AVG EQUALS 2 VM OVER PI TIMES COS ALPHA]
    A3 --> F3[V0 AVG EQUALS 3 ROOT 3 VM OVER 2 PI TIMES COS ALPHA]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Always draw the **output voltage waveform** (V₀ vs ωt) showing:
> 1. The firing instant (α) marked explicitly.
> 2. The conduction interval highlighted.
> 3. The natural commutation points (for 3-φ) at 30°, 150°, 270°.
> 
> A correctly labelled waveform often earns **2–3 easy marks** in 14-mark questions.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution as per KTU 2024 Scheme (ESE Pattern):**
> - Part A: Short answer (3 marks each, ~1 page limit)
> - Part B: Descriptive (14 marks each, 2 sub-parts of 7 marks each, **internal choice** between Q1 and Q2 — they cover **alternate** topics)

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model 2A]**
**Q: Define firing angle and conduction angle for a thyristor. Why is the firing angle measured from the natural commutation point?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> - **Firing Angle (α):** It is the angle (measured in electrical degrees) between the instant the thyristor could have started conducting (natural commutation point) and the instant it is actually triggered by the gate pulse. **(1 mark)**
> - **Conduction Angle (γ):** It is the angle during which the thyristor is in the ON state and conducting current. For a full converter, γ = π − α. **(1 mark)**
> - **Why measured from natural commutation point:** The natural commutation point is the instant when the incoming phase voltage becomes greater than the outgoing phase voltage, and a diode would naturally start conducting. Measuring α from this point provides a **uniform reference** for all phases in multi-pulse converters and simplifies the integration limits in the V₀ formula. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024, Model 2A]**
**Q: Distinguish between a half-controlled and a fully-controlled bridge rectifier. State one application of each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | Half-Controlled (Semi-converter) | Fully-Controlled (Full-converter) |
> |---------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
> | Devices | 2 SCRs + 2 Diodes | 4 SCRs |
> | Output Voltage | Always positive (unipolar) | Can be positive or negative (bipolar) |
> | Inversion Mode | **Not possible** | **Possible** (for α > 90°) |
> | Quadrant Operation | **1st quadrant only** | **All 4 quadrants** (inversion possible) |
> | Application | Battery charger, DC welding | DC motor drive with regenerative braking |
> 
> **(3 marks — 1 for each distinguishing row + 0.5 for application of each)**

---

## PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Choice Format:** KTU gives you a choice between Q1(a) and Q1(b) — they are *alternative* problems, not sub-parts of the same problem. Below, **Question A** and **Question B** are the two choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — 1-Phase Full Converter Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model 2B]**
A single-phase fully controlled bridge rectifier is supplied from a **230 V, 50 Hz** AC source. The load consists of **R = 15 Ω** in series with a large inductance L such that the load current is **constant and continuous at 8 A**. The firing angle is **α = 45°**.

**Solve the following:**

**(a) [7 marks] Draw the circuit diagram and the output voltage waveform. Derive the expression for average output voltage. Hence calculate the average output voltage for the given data.**

**(b) [7 marks] Calculate the RMS output voltage, RMS supply current, and the power delivered to the load. Also compute the supply power factor.**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

*Circuit:* A 1-φ H-bridge with thyristors T1, T2 (positive group) and T3, T4 (negative group), fed by AC source Vs, connected to R-L load.

*Waveform:* The output voltage V₀ is a rectified sinusoid shifted by α. It equals +Vm sin(ωt) from α to (π + α) [T1–T2 conduct], and +Vm sin(ωt) from (π + α) to (2π + α) [T3–T4 conduct]. The waveform has period π.

*Derivation:*

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{1}{\pi}\int_{\alpha}^{\pi+\alpha}V_m \sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = \frac{V_m}{\pi}\big[\cos\alpha - \cos(\pi+\alpha)\big] = \frac{2V_m}{\pi}\cos\alpha
$$

**Step-by-step:**

1. Set up integral over the conduction window α to (π + α). **(1 mark)**
2. Evaluate the antiderivative −cos(ωt). **(1 mark)**
3. Apply limits to obtain cos α − cos(π + α). **(1 mark)**
4. Use the identity cos(π + α) = −cos α to simplify. **(1 mark)**
5. State the final formula. **(0.5 mark)**
6. Waveform with α marked. **(1 mark)**
7. Numerical calculation: Vm = 325.27 V, V₀,avg = (2 × 325.27 / π) × cos 45° = 207.07 × 0.7071 ≈ **146.4 V**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b):**

1. **RMS Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{0,rms} = V_m\sqrt{\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\sin(2\alpha)}{2\pi}} = 325.27 \sqrt{0.5 + \frac{\sin 90°}{2\pi}} = 325.27 \sqrt{0.5 + 0.1592} = 325.27 \times 0.8132 \approx 264.5 \, \text{V}
$$

**(2 marks)**

2. **RMS Supply Current:** Since the load current is constant at I₀ = 8 A, the supply current is a quasi-square wave of amplitude 8 A. For a 1-φ full bridge with constant load current:

$$
I_{s,rms} = I_0 = 8 \, \text{A}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

3. **Power Delivered:**

$$
P = I_0^2 R = 8^2 \times 15 = 960 \, \text{W}
$$

**(1.5 marks)**

4. **Supply Power Factor:**

Apparent power: S = V_s,rms × I_s,rms = 230 × 8 = 1840 VA

$$
\cos\phi = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{960}{1840} \approx 0.5217 \,\, (\text{lagging})
$$

**(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — 3-Phase Half Wave Focus**

**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2023, Model 2B]**
A three-phase half-wave controlled rectifier is supplied from a **3-phase, 400 V (line-to-line), 50 Hz** source. The load is highly inductive with **R = 8 Ω**, and the current is continuous. The firing angle is **α = 30°**.

**Solve the following:**

**(a) [7 marks] Explain the working principle with a neat circuit diagram and waveform. Derive the average output voltage expression.**

**(b) [7 marks] Calculate (i) average output voltage, (ii) average output current, and (iii) the firing angle required to obtain an average output voltage of 200 V.**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

*Working Principle:*
- Three thyristors T1, T2, T3 are connected to phases R, Y, B with their cathodes joined to the positive load terminal.
- At any instant, only the thyristor connected to the highest phase voltage conducts.
- Natural commutation occurs at 30°, 150°, 270°. With thyristors, conduction is delayed by α.
- T1 conducts from (30° + α) to (150° + α); T2 from (150° + α) to (270° + α); T3 from (270° + α) to (30° + α + 360°). **(2 marks)**

*Derivation:*

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3}{2\pi}\int_{\pi/6+\alpha}^{5\pi/6+\alpha} V_m \sin(\omega t)\,d(\omega t) = \frac{3V_m}{2\pi}\big[\cos(\pi/6+\alpha) - \cos(5\pi/6+\alpha)\big]
$$

Using sum-to-product formula:
$$
\cos(\pi/6+\alpha) - \cos(5\pi/6+\alpha) = 2\sin(90°+\alpha)\sin(60°) = \sqrt{3}\cos\alpha
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3}\,V_m}{2\pi}\cos\alpha}
$$

**Marks breakdown for (a):**
1. Circuit diagram and explanation: **(2 marks)**
2. Waveform with commutation points: **(1.5 marks)**
3. Integral setup and evaluation: **(2 marks)**
4. Application of trig identity and final result: **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b):**

*Given:* V_LL,rms = 400 V → V_LN,rms = 400/√3 = 230.94 V → Vm = 326.60 V; R = 8 Ω; α = 30°.

**(i) Average Output Voltage:**

$$
V_{0,avg} = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 326.60}{2\pi}\cos 30° = 270.13 \times 0.8660 \approx 233.93 \, \text{V}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Average Output Current:**

$$
I_{0,avg} = \frac{V_{0,avg}}{R} = \frac{233.93}{8} \approx 29.24 \, \text{A}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iii) Firing angle for V₀ = 200 V:**

$$
200 = \frac{3\sqrt{3} \times 326.60}{2\pi}\cos\alpha_{new} = 270.13 \cos\alpha_{new}
$$

$$
\cos\alpha_{new} = \frac{200}{270.13} = 0.7404 \implies \alpha_{new} = \cos^{-1}(0.7404) \approx 42.27°
$$

**(3 marks)**

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (Module 2 — Controlled Rectifiers)**
>
> 1. **Wrong Vm reference:** Using V_LL (line voltage) instead of V_LN (phase voltage) in the 3-φ half-wave V₀ formula. Always convert first: Vm = √2 × V_LN,rms = √(2/3) × V_LL,rms.
> 
> 2. **Ignoring freewheeling:** In the semi-converter with R-load, students often forget that V₀ = 0 from π to (π + α). This gives an incorrect (higher) average.
> 
> 3. **Continuous vs Discontinuous:** The formula V₀,avg = (2Vm/π) cos α is **ONLY valid for continuous conduction** (large L). With insufficient L, the current becomes discontinuous and the formula is invalid.
> 
> 4. **Sign convention for Inversion:** When α > 90°, V₀ becomes negative — students often forget the negative sign and write the magnitude only. The negative sign is essential for marks in inverter-mode questions.
> 
> 5. **Units in RMS calculation:** When asked for "RMS output voltage", the answer must be in **Volts RMS**, not peak. Always confirm the question's requirement.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting "natural commutation point" in 3-φ:** The 30°, 150°, 270° natural commutation points are the reference for α. Marking α from 0° (zero crossing) is a common conceptual error worth losing 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist before your exam — it covers every must-know from Module 2.**

### 1. Topologies Covered
- ☐ 1-φ Half-Controlled Bridge (Semi-converter) — 2 SCR + 2 Diode
- ☐ 1-φ Fully-Controlled Bridge (Full-converter) — 4 SCR
- ☐ 3-φ Half-Wave Controlled Rectifier (3-pulse) — 3 SCR

### 2. Master Formulas (Memorize These)
- ☐ 1-φ Semi (R-Load): V₀,avg = (Vm/π)(1 + cos α)
- ☐ 1-φ Full (RL-CCM): V₀,avg = (2Vm/π) cos α
- ☐ 3-φ Half-Wave (RL-CCM): V₀,avg = (3√3 Vm / 2π) cos α ≈ 0.827 Vm cos α
- ☐ 1-φ Full RMS: V₀,rms = Vm · √[1/2 + sin(2α)/(2π)]
- ☐ Maximum V₀ values: 1-φ = 0.637 Vm; 3-φ half-wave = 0.827 Vm

### 3. Firing Angle Limits
- ☐ Rectification: 0° ≤ α ≤ 90° (V₀ > 0)
- ☐ Inversion: 90° < α ≤ 180° (V₀ < 0, only full-bridge topologies)
- ☐ α = 90° gives V₀,avg = 0

### 4. Key Concepts
- ☐ **Natural commutation point** = the instant the incoming phase voltage equals the outgoing one.
- ☐ **Freewheeling** in semi-converter occurs through D2–D4 (or D1–D3) when supply reverses.
- ☐ **Continuous conduction** requires L > R/(ω · tan α) approximately.
- ☐ **3-φ has 3-pulse output**, ripple frequency = 3f = 150 Hz.
- ☐ **Power factor** of all phase-controlled rectifiers is **lagging and poor**, especially at high α.

### 5. Quick Problem-Solving Steps
1. Identify topology (semi / full / 3-φ half-wave).
2. Compute Vm = √2 × V_rms (use V_LN for 3-φ).
3. Apply the appropriate V₀,avg formula.
4. For current: I₀,avg = V₀,avg / R.
5. For power: P = I²R (constant current) or P = V₀,rms × I₀,rms (apparent).

### 6. Common Numerical Values
- ☐ Vm for 230 V rms = 325.27 V
- ☐ 2Vm/π = 207.07 V (max 1-φ full-bridge output)
- ☐ 3√3 Vm / 2π = 0.827 Vm (max 3-φ half-wave output)
- ☐ cos 30° = 0.866, cos 45° = 0.707, cos 60° = 0.5

### 7. KTU-Specific Watch-Outs
- ☐ Always draw the **output waveform** showing α and conduction period.
- ☐ State the **assumptions**: ideal SCRs, continuous conduction (where applicable).
- ☐ Mention the **devices used** (SCR for thyristor) in circuit descriptions.
- ☐ Use **proper SI units** in every step.
- ☐ In multi-part questions, **label each sub-part answer** clearly.

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Power Electronics (OEEVT613) | KTU 2024 Scheme**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
