topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT613 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Buck

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 : Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BUCK CONVERTER (Step-Down Chopper)**
> A **Buck Converter** is a DC-to-DC switched-mode power converter (SMPC) in which the **average output voltage** is always **less than** the **input DC source voltage**. It is realized by **chopping** the input DC voltage using a high-frequency semiconductor switch, and then filtering the chopped waveform through a **low-pass LC filter** so that a smoothed, ripple-controlled lower DC voltage is delivered to the load.

The ratio of output to input voltage in the ideal (lossless) case is governed solely by the **duty cycle** $D$ of the active switch:

$$V_{o} = D \cdot V_{s}$$

where $D = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{s}}$ and $T_{s}$ is the switching period of the chopper.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 3):** This topic belongs to the broader module covering *DC-DC Converters (Choppers)*. Within the KTU 2024 scheme, the buck converter is treated as the foundational topology from which **boost** and **buck-boost** converters are conceptually derived by re-arranging the same five components (switch, diode, inductor, capacitor, source).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **water tap connected to a high-pressure water tank**:
- The **tank pressure** represents the **input DC source** $V_{s}$.
- The **tap** (when opened, water rushes out; when closed, no water flows) represents the **transistor switch**.
- A **leaky bucket with a small drain hole** placed after the tap represents the **LC filter** (inductor stores water energy like kinetic inertia, capacitor acts as a small pressure reservoir).
- The **water flowing steadily out of the drain** represents the **smoothed DC output voltage** $V_{o}$.

**Key Insight:** The tap is *never* left half-open (that would waste energy as heat, like a linear regulator). Instead, it is **fully ON for a short time** and **fully OFF for a longer time**, switching very fast. The bucket averages out these bursts of water into a smooth, lower-pressure stream. The longer the tap is ON (higher $D$), the higher the output pressure; the shorter the ON-time (lower $D$), the lower the output pressure — but **always lower** than the tank pressure.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Design Metrics

The following quantities are fundamental to the buck converter and must be **memorized** for KTU exams:

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_{s}$ | Input DC Source Voltage | **Volts (V)** |
| $V_{o}$ | Average Output Voltage | **Volts (V)** |
| $I_{o}$ | Average Load Current | **Amperes (A)** |
| $I_{L}$ | Inductor Current (average) | **Amperes (A)** |
| $L$ | Filter Inductance | **Henries (H)** |
| $C$ | Filter Capacitance | **Farads (F)** |
| $R$ | Load Resistance | **Ohms ($\Omega$)** |
| $f_{s}$ | Switching Frequency | **Hertz (Hz)** |
| $T_{s}$ | Switching Period = $1/f_{s}$ | **Seconds (s)** |
| $D$ | Duty Cycle (0 $\lt$ D $\lt$ 1) | **Dimensionless** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Remember:** In an *ideal* buck converter, the **switch** and **diode** are assumed lossless ($V_{drop} = 0$ when ON, $I_{leak} = 0$ when OFF). The inductor and capacitor are also assumed ideal (no parasitic resistance). These assumptions simplify the KTU derivations significantly.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 : Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Two Sub-Intervals per Switching Cycle

The buck converter has exactly **two topological states** per switching period in continuous conduction mode (CCM):

**Sub-Interval 1 : Switch ON (Duration = $D \cdot T_{s}$)**
- The MOSFET/IGBT switch $S$ is closed.
- The diode $D$ is reverse-biased and OFF.
- The input source $V_{s}$ is connected directly across the **series combination** of inductor $L$ and load $R$ (with $C$ in parallel with $R$).
- The voltage across the inductor is $V_{L} = V_{s} - V_{o} > 0$.
- Inductor current **ramps UP linearly** (energy stored in magnetic field).
- Capacitor charges toward $V_{o}$.

**Sub-Interval 2 : Switch OFF (Duration = $(1-D) \cdot T_{s}$)**
- The switch $S$ is open.
- The diode $D$ becomes forward-biased (freewheeling action) and conducts.
- The inductor opposes the sudden change in current, reversing its polarity to maintain current flow.
- The voltage across the inductor is $V_{L} = -V_{o} < 0$.
- Inductor current **ramps DOWN linearly** (energy released to load).
- Capacitor discharges into the load to maintain $V_{o}$ during this interval.

### 2.2 Volt-Second Balance (The Heart of the Buck Converter)

The **fundamental principle** of any DC-DC converter in steady state is the **Volt-Second Balance** of the inductor:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volt-Second Balance Theorem:** In steady-state operation, the **average voltage across the inductor over one complete switching cycle must be zero**, because any net DC voltage would cause the inductor current to ramp up indefinitely, violating energy conservation.

Mathematically, this is written as:

$$\int_{0}^{T_{s}} v_{L}(t) \, dt = 0$$

For a buck converter in CCM, this gives the canonical relation:

$$(V_{s} - V_{o}) \cdot D \cdot T_{s} + (-V_{o}) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T_{s} = 0$$

Solving yields the **ideal (lossless) voltage transfer ratio**:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = D}$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes **every formula** you must know to solve KTU problems on the buck converter. Master these before attempting any exam question.

| Parameter | Formula | Boundary Condition / Note |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Duty Cycle | $D = \dfrac{V_{o}}{V_{s}}$ | Valid only for CCM, ideal converter |
| Voltage Transfer Ratio | $M = \dfrac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = D$ | Always $\lt 1$ (step-down) |
| Average Inductor Current | $I_{L,avg} = I_{o}$ | Equals load current in CCM |
| Inductor Current Ripple (peak-to-peak) | $\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{o}(1-D)}{f_{s} L}$ | Equivalent: $\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{(V_{s} - V_{o}) D}{f_{s} L}$ |
| Minimum Inductance for CCM | $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_{s}}$ | Boundary of CCM/DCM |
| Critical Load Resistance | $R_{crit} = \dfrac{2 f_{s} L}{1-D}$ | If $R \gt R_{crit} \Rightarrow$ DCM |
| Average Diode Current | $I_{D,avg} = (1-D) I_{o}$ | Diode conducts in OFF state |
| RMS Switch Current | $I_{S,rms} = I_{o}\sqrt{D}$ | For thermal design |
| Average Switch Current | $I_{S,avg} = D \cdot I_{o}$ | For conduction loss |
| Output Voltage Ripple (peak-to-peak) | $\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{o}(1-D)}{8 L C f_{s}^{2}}$ | Valid for $\Delta V_{o}/V_{o} \ll 1$ |
| Capacitor Voltage Ripple (peak-to-peak) | $\Delta V_{C} = \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{8 f_{s} C}$ | Same form for $\Delta V_{o}$ |
| Switching Period | $T_{s} = \dfrac{1}{f_{s}} = D T_{s} + (1-D) T_{s}$ | Fundamental relation |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** In the formula $\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{o}(1-D)}{f_{s} L}$, the expression $\vert V_{o} \vert$ uses absolute value notation. When rendering the formula in your answer sheet, write it with vertical bars. **In this PDF/LaTeX format, do not use the bare pipe character** — always use $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ to avoid markdown table corruption.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The buck converter is **the most widely manufactured power electronic circuit in the world**. It is found in:

- **Laptop / mobile chargers:** Step down $V_{s} = 19\text{ V}$ (or $V_{s} = 12\text{ V}$) from an adapter to $V_{o} = 3.3\text{ V}$, $5\text{ V}$, or $V_{o} = 1.2\text{ V}$ for the CPU.
- **DC motor drives (armature control):** Adjust the armature voltage to control speed below base speed.
- **Battery Management Systems (BMS):** Step down a higher battery pack voltage to power auxiliary systems.
- **LED drivers:** Regulate constant current/voltage to high-brightness LEDs.
- **Point-of-Load (POL) converters** in servers and telecom equipment.
- **Solar PV systems:** Interface the variable PV panel output to a fixed DC bus or battery.
- **Electric Vehicle (EV) auxiliary systems:** Power 12 V accessories from a 400 V traction battery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why buck converters dominate modern power electronics:** They achieve efficiencies of **90% to 98%** because the switch and diode are either fully ON or fully OFF — they never operate in the linear region where power is dissipated as heat. The energy that *would* have been lost is instead stored temporarily in the inductor's magnetic field and released to the load, achieving near-lossless voltage conversion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 : Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Voltage Transfer Ratio ($V_{o}/V_{s}$) using Volt-Second Balance

**Given:** A buck converter operating in CCM with input voltage $V_{s}$, output voltage $V_{o}$, duty cycle $D$, switching period $T_{s}$.

**To Find:** The relationship between $V_{o}$, $V_{s}$, and $D$.

**Step 1 — Identify the inductor voltage waveform in each sub-interval.**

During **Sub-Interval 1** (Switch ON, duration $= D \cdot T_{s}$):
Applying KVL around the loop (source → switch → inductor → load), the inductor voltage is:

$$v_{L,1} = V_{s} - V_{o}$$

During **Sub-Interval 2** (Switch OFF, diode ON, duration $= (1-D) \cdot T_{s}$):
The diode freewheels; the source is disconnected. The inductor sees only the load voltage (with reversed polarity):

$$v_{L,2} = -V_{o}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Volt-Second Balance theorem.**

The area under the inductor voltage waveform over one full cycle must sum to zero (in steady state):

$$\int_{0}^{D T_{s}} v_{L,1} \, dt + \int_{D T_{s}}^{T_{s}} v_{L,2} \, dt = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise constant voltages.**

$$\int_{0}^{D T_{s}} (V_{s} - V_{o}) \, dt + \int_{D T_{s}}^{T_{s}} (-V_{o}) \, dt = 0$$

Since the integrands are constant in each interval, integration reduces to multiplication by the interval length:

$$(V_{s} - V_{o}) \cdot D T_{s} + (-V_{o}) \cdot (1 - D) T_{s} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $T_{s}$ to simplify.**

$$(V_{s} - V_{o}) D - V_{o} (1 - D) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Expand and collect terms in $V_{o}$.**

$$V_{s} D - V_{o} D - V_{o} + V_{o} D = 0$$

$$V_{s} D - V_{o} = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $V_{o}$.**

$$\boxed{V_{o} = D \cdot V_{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the most important derivation in the entire buck-converter module.** It is the **starting point** for every numerical problem. Always derive this first in your answer sheet, even if the question gives it directly — it earns you **2 marks** automatically on the KTU valuation key.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Inductor Current Ripple ($\Delta I_{L}$)

**Given:** Buck converter with $V_{s}$, $V_{o}$, $D$, $L$, $f_{s}$ in CCM.

**To Find:** Peak-to-peak inductor current ripple $\Delta I_{L}$.

**Step 1 — Recall the inductor V-I relationship.**

For an inductor:

$$v_{L} = L \dfrac{di_{L}}{dt} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta i_{L} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int v_{L} \, dt = \dfrac{v_{L} \cdot \Delta t}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply to Sub-Interval 1 (Switch ON).**

In this interval, $v_{L} = V_{s} - V_{o}$ and $\Delta t = D T_{s}$. The current rises from its minimum to its maximum:

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{(V_{s} - V_{o}) \cdot D T_{s}}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of $V_{o}$ using $V_{o} = D V_{s}$.**

$$V_{s} - V_{o} = V_{s} - D V_{s} = V_{s}(1 - D)$$

Also, $D T_{s} = \dfrac{V_{o} T_{s}}{V_{s}}$.

Substitute:

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{s}(1-D)}{L} \cdot \dfrac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} \cdot T_{s} = \dfrac{V_{o}(1-D) T_{s}}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Replace $T_{s}$ with $1/f_{s}$.**

$$\boxed{\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{L f_{s}}}$$

This is the **canonical KTU formula** for inductor ripple current. You will use it in nearly every design problem.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Output Voltage Ripple ($\Delta V_{o}$)

**Given:** Buck converter with $V_{o}$, $D$, $L$, $C$, $f_{s}$ in CCM.

**To Find:** Peak-to-peak output voltage ripple $\Delta V_{o}$.

**Step 1 — Recognize that the capacitor supplies the difference between the load current and the inductor current.**

The capacitor charges when $i_{L} > i_{o}$ and discharges when $i_{L} < i_{o}$. The total **charge** entering/leaving the capacitor per half-cycle equals the area of the triangular $\Delta i_{L}$ waveform above the average current line:

$$\Delta Q = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{T_{s}}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{2} = \dfrac{\Delta I_{L} \cdot T_{s}}{8}$$

**Step 2 — Relate $\Delta Q$ to the voltage ripple via the capacitor equation.**

$$Q = C \cdot V \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{8 f_{s} C}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expression for $\Delta I_{L}$ from Derivation 2.**

$$\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{1}{8 f_{s} C} \cdot \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{L f_{s}}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{8 L C f_{s}^{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** To reduce output voltage ripple, the designer can: (a) increase $L$, (b) increase $C$, (c) increase $f_{s}$, or (d) operate near $D \to 0$ or $D \to 1$ where the $(1-D)$ factor is small. In practice, raising $f_{s}$ is the most cost-effective strategy because it shrinks $L$ and $C$ simultaneously.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Critical (Minimum) Inductance for CCM

**Given:** Buck converter in CCM with load $R$, switching frequency $f_{s}$, duty cycle $D$.

**To Find:** $L_{min}$, the smallest inductance that keeps the converter in CCM at the boundary of conduction.

**Step 1 — Boundary condition definition.**

At the boundary of CCM, the inductor current **just touches zero** at the end of Sub-Interval 2:

$$I_{L,min} = I_{L,avg} - \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{2} = 0$$

Therefore:

$$I_{L,avg} = \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Express $I_{L,avg}$ in terms of load.**

In a buck converter, all the load current flows through the inductor on average:

$$I_{L,avg} = I_{o} = \dfrac{V_{o}}{R}$$

**Step 3 — Express $\Delta I_{L}$ at the boundary.**

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{o}(1-D)}{L f_{s}}$$

**Step 4 — Set $I_{L,avg} = \Delta I_{L}/2$.**

$$\dfrac{V_{o}}{R} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{o}(1-D)}{L_{min} f_{s}}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $V_{o}$ and solve for $L_{min}$.**

$$L_{min} = \dfrac{R (1-D)}{2 f_{s}}$$

$$\boxed{L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_{s}}}$$

**Step 6 — Alternative: Critical Load Resistance $R_{crit}$.**

Sometimes the question is phrased the other way: *for a given $L$ and $f_{s}$, what is the maximum load resistance that still keeps the converter in CCM?*

$$R_{crit} = \dfrac{2 f_{s} L}{1 - D}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU's most-tested concept):**
> - If $L \gt L_{min}$ (equivalently $R \lt R_{crit}$): **CCM**
> - If $L \lt L_{min}$ (equivalently $R \gt R_{crit}$): **DCM**
> - If $L = L_{min}$ (equivalently $R = R_{crit}$): **Boundary / Critical Conduction**

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem)

> **Problem:** A buck converter has $V_{s} = 24\text{ V}$, $L = 20\text{ }\mu\text{H}$, $C = 100\text{ }\mu\text{F}$, $R = 5\text{ }\Omega$, and $f_{s} = 100\text{ kHz}$. The duty cycle is $D = 0.4$. Determine: (a) the average output voltage, (b) the average inductor current, (c) the inductor current ripple, and (d) the output voltage ripple. Verify whether the converter operates in CCM.

**Solution:**

**(a) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{o} = D \cdot V_{s} = 0.4 \times 24 = 9.6 \text{ V}$$

**(b) Average Inductor Current (= Load Current in CCM):**

$$I_{L,avg} = \dfrac{V_{o}}{R} = \dfrac{9.6}{5} = 1.92 \text{ A}$$

**(c) Inductor Current Ripple:**

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{L f_{s}} = \dfrac{9.6 \times (1 - 0.4)}{20 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{9.6 \times 0.6}{2.0} = \dfrac{5.76}{2.0} = 2.88 \text{ A}$$

**Check CCM condition:**

$$I_{L,min} = I_{L,avg} - \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{2} = 1.92 - 1.44 = 0.48 \text{ A} \gt 0$$

Since $I_{L,min} \gt 0$, the converter **operates in CCM**. ✓

**(d) Output Voltage Ripple:**

$$\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{8 L C f_{s}^{2}} = \dfrac{9.6 \times 0.6}{8 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (100 \times 10^{3})^{2}}$$

Compute the denominator step by step:

$$8 \times 20 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1600 \times 10^{-11} = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$(100 \times 10^{3})^{2} = 10^{10}$$

Denominator $= 1.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 10^{10} = 160$

$$\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{5.76}{160} = 0.036 \text{ V} = 36 \text{ mV}$$

**Percentage Ripple:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_{o}}{V_{o}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.036}{9.6} \times 100\% = 0.375\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A 0.375% ripple is very low and acceptable for most digital/analog loads. This confirms the design is well-suited for CCM operation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 : Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Power Circuit Schematic of a Buck Converter

The buck converter consists of **five essential components** connected in a specific topology. The diagram below shows the **functional architecture and current-flow paths** during both modes of operation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc["DC SOURCE Vs"] --> nodeSw["SWITCH S MOSFET or IGBT"]
    nodeSw --> nodeL["INDUCTOR L"]
    nodeL --> nodeOut["OUTPUT NODE Vout"]
    nodeOut --> nodeR["LOAD R"]
    nodeR --> nodeG["GROUND"]
    nodeG --> nodeSrc
    nodeOut --> nodeC["CAPACITOR C"]
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD["DIODE D Freewheeling"] --> nodeOut
    nodeL --> nodeD
    nodeCtrl["PWM GATING SIGNAL Duty Cycle D"] -.-> nodeSw
    classDef sourceStyle fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef switchStyle fill:#a7f3d0,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef inductorStyle fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef diodeStyle fill:#fecaca,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef capStyle fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#6b21a8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef outStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#78350f,stroke-width:3px,color:#000000
    classDef ctrlStyle fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class nodeSrc sourceStyle
    class nodeSw switchStyle
    class nodeL inductorStyle
    class nodeD diodeStyle
    class nodeC capStyle
    class nodeR outStyle
    class nodeOut outStyle
    class nodeG outStyle
    class nodeCtrl ctrlStyle
```

**Key Connection Rules (Memorize for KTU):**
1. **Switch** is in series with the source.
2. **Diode** is in parallel with the (inductor + load) branch, with **cathode** at the output node and **anode** at ground.
3. **Inductor** is in series with the load.
4. **Capacitor** is in parallel with the load.
5. The **diode direction** determines whether the circuit is a buck, boost, or buck-boost. For a buck, the diode's **anode is grounded and cathode faces the inductor**, ensuring it freewheels when the switch opens.

### 4.2 Mode-of-Operation State Machine

The converter alternates between two topological states. The diagram below clarifies the **current paths** in each state.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ModeOn : t = 0
    ModeOn : SWITCH ON DIODE OFF
    ModeOn : Current Path Vs to S to L to Load to Ground
    ModeOn : Inductor Charges vL equals Vs minus Vo
    ModeOn : Inductor Current Ramps UP
    ModeOn --> ModeOff : t equals D Ts
    ModeOff : SWITCH OFF DIODE ON
    ModeOff : Current Path Inductor to Load to Ground to Diode
    ModeOff : Freewheeling Action
    ModeOff : vL equals minus Vo
    ModeOff : Inductor Current Ramps DOWN
    ModeOff --> ModeOn : t equals Ts Cycle Repeats
```

### 4.3 CCM vs DCM — Conceptual Block Diagram

The boundary between **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** and **Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM)** is governed by the **relative magnitudes of $L$, $R$, and $f_{s}$**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["Buck Converter Steady State"] --> nodeB{"Is ILmin greater than zero?"}
    nodeB -- "YES" --> nodeC["CCM Continuous Conduction Mode"]
    nodeB -- "EQUAL TO ZERO" --> nodeD["BOUNDARY MODE Critical Conduction"]
    nodeB -- "NO" --> nodeE["DCM Discontinuous Conduction Mode"]
    nodeC --> nodeF["Vo equals D Vs simple relation"]
    nodeD --> nodeG["ILmin equals zero boundary condition"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["Vo depends on D L R fs more complex"]
    nodeF --> nodeI["Apply Lmin equals R times 1 minus D divided by 2 fs"]
    nodeH --> nodeJ["Three sub intervals per cycle"]
    classDef ccmStyle fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef dcmStyle fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef boundaryStyle fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef startStyle fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class nodeA startStyle
    class nodeC ccmStyle
    class nodeD boundaryStyle
    class nodeE dcmStyle
```

### 4.4 Inductor Current and Output Voltage Waveforms (CCM)

This block diagram summarizes the **time-domain waveforms** that the KTU examiner will expect you to draw in a 7-mark problem.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodePWM["PWM Gating Signal Vgs to Switch S"] --> nodeIL["Inductor Current iL t Triangular Wave"]
    nodePWM --> nodeVC["Capacitor Voltage vc t Approximately DC with Small Ripple"]
    nodePWM --> nodeVD["Diode Voltage VD Zero when ON Equals Vs when OFF"]
    nodePWM --> nodeVS["Switch Voltage Vds Zero when ON Equals Vs when OFF"]
    nodeIL -- "Average IL avg equals Io" --> nodeSummary["Key Observations Slope plus Delta IL divided by D Ts Slope minus Delta IL divided by 1 minus D times Ts"]
    nodeVC -- "Ripple amplitude Delta Vo" --> nodeSummary
    classDef sigStyle fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    classDef sumStyle fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000
    class nodePWM sigStyle
    class nodeIL sigStyle
    class nodeVC sigStyle
    class nodeVD sigStyle
    class nodeVS sigStyle
    class nodeSummary sumStyle
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Drawing Instructions for the Exam:**
> 1. Draw the gate pulse as a square wave (high for $D T_{s}$, low for $(1-D) T_{s}$).
> 2. Draw $i_{L}$ as a triangular wave (rising during ON, falling during OFF) with $I_{L,avg}$ as the centerline.
> 3. Mark the maximum and minimum points: $I_{L,max} = I_{L,avg} + \Delta I_{L}/2$ and $I_{L,min} = I_{L,avg} - \Delta I_{L}/2$.
> 4. Draw $V_{o}$ as a flat line with a small triangular ripple of amplitude $\Delta V_{o}/2$ on either side.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 : KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. (3 Marks)** Define a buck converter. State its key voltage transfer ratio in continuous conduction mode.

**Model Answer:**
A buck converter is a switched-mode DC-to-DC converter whose average output voltage is **lower than** the input DC source voltage. It achieves this by **chopping** the input DC voltage with a high-frequency switch and then **filtering** the chopped waveform using an LC low-pass filter to produce a smooth DC output. In continuous conduction mode (CCM), with ideal components, the voltage transfer ratio is:

$$\dfrac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = D$$

where $D$ is the duty cycle of the switch, $0 \lt D \lt 1$. Hence the output is always less than the input, confirming the **step-down** nature of the converter.

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
> **Q2. (3 Marks)** Distinguish between Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) and Discontinuous Conduction Mode (DCM) in a buck converter.

**Model Answer:**
The two conduction modes are distinguished by the **behavior of the inductor current** $i_{L}(t)$ over one switching cycle:

- **CCM (Continuous Conduction Mode):** The inductor current $i_{L}(t)$ **never falls to zero** during the switching cycle. The current is always positive ($I_{L,min} \gt 0$), and the diode freewheels for the entire OFF-time. CCM occurs when the inductance is large enough (or the load resistance is small enough).

- **DCM (Discontinuous Conduction Mode):** The inductor current $i_{L}(t)$ **falls to zero and remains at zero** for a finite portion of the switching cycle. The diode stops conducting before the next switching cycle begins. DCM occurs when the inductance is too small (or the load is too light), causing the inductor's stored energy to deplete fully before the next ON-pulse.

The **boundary** between them is defined by $L_{min} = \dfrac{(1-D) R}{2 f_{s}}$.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q3A. (14 Marks)** A buck converter operates with the following specifications: $V_{s} = 48 \text{ V}$, switching frequency $f_{s} = 25 \text{ kHz}$, duty cycle $D = 0.3$, load resistance $R = 10 \text{ }\Omega$, filter inductance $L = 200 \text{ }\mu\text{H}$, and filter capacitance $C = 100 \text{ }\mu\text{F}$. Assume ideal components.
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the voltage transfer ratio of the buck converter in CCM and compute the output voltage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Calculate the average inductor current, the inductor current ripple, and the output voltage ripple. Verify whether the converter operates in CCM. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of Voltage Transfer Ratio:**

During Sub-Interval 1 (Switch ON, duration $D T_{s}$), the inductor voltage is $v_{L,1} = V_{s} - V_{o}$.

During Sub-Interval 2 (Switch OFF, duration $(1-D) T_{s}$), the inductor voltage is $v_{L,2} = -V_{o}$.

Applying volt-second balance over one switching cycle:

$$(V_{s} - V_{o}) \cdot D T_{s} + (-V_{o}) \cdot (1 - D) T_{s} = 0$$

$$V_{s} D - V_{o} D - V_{o} + V_{o} D = 0$$

$$V_{s} D - V_{o} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{V_{o}}{V_{s}} = D}$$

Substituting the given numerical values:

$$V_{o} = 0.3 \times 48 = 14.4 \text{ V}$$

**Part (b) — Current, Ripple, and CCM Verification:**

Average inductor current (equals load current in CCM):

$$I_{L,avg} = \dfrac{V_{o}}{R} = \dfrac{14.4}{10} = 1.44 \text{ A}$$

Inductor current ripple (peak-to-peak):

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{L f_{s}} = \dfrac{14.4 \times (1 - 0.3)}{200 \times 10^{-6} \times 25 \times 10^{3}}$$

$$\Delta I_{L} = \dfrac{14.4 \times 0.7}{5.0} = \dfrac{10.08}{5.0} = 2.016 \text{ A}$$

Check CCM condition — minimum inductor current:

$$I_{L,min} = I_{L,avg} - \dfrac{\Delta I_{L}}{2} = 1.44 - \dfrac{2.016}{2} = 1.44 - 1.008 = 0.432 \text{ A}$$

Since $I_{L,min} = 0.432 \text{ A} \gt 0$, the converter **operates in CCM**. ✓

Output voltage ripple (peak-to-peak):

$$\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{V_{o} (1 - D)}{8 L C f_{s}^{2}} = \dfrac{14.4 \times 0.7}{8 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} \times (25 \times 10^{3})^{2}}$$

Denominator computation:

$$8 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 1600 \times 10^{-11} = 1.6 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$(25 \times 10^{3})^{2} = 625 \times 10^{6} = 6.25 \times 10^{8}$$

Denominator $= 1.6 \times 10^{-8} \times 6.25 \times 10^{8} = 10.0$

$$\Delta V_{o} = \dfrac{10.08}{10.0} = 1.008 \text{ V}$$

Percentage ripple:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_{o}}{V_{o}} \times 100\% = \dfrac{1.008}{14.4} \times 100\% = 7\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> 1. **Do not forget units.** Writing "$V_{o} = 14.4$" without the unit "V" loses **0.5 mark**.
> 2. **The $T_{s}$ substitution is mandatory.** Many students forget to convert $f_{s}$ to $T_{s} = 1/f_{s}$ in the ripple formula, producing an answer off by a factor of 10 or 100. This is the **#1 reason students lose 2-3 marks** on this type of question.
> 3. **The CCM verification is not optional.** Even if the question doesn't explicitly ask for it, the examiner checks the boundary condition. Skipping it can cost **1 mark** from the "design verification" rubric.
> 4. **State the assumption of ideal components** at the top of the answer. This earns 0.5-1 mark for "understanding the problem context."

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Model Question 2024 Scheme]**
> **Q3B. (14 Marks)** [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]
>
> **(a)** With the aid of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a buck converter in continuous conduction mode. Show the current paths in both modes and derive the expression for the output voltage. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** For a buck converter with $V_{s} = 100 \text{ V}$, $D = 0.6$, $f_{s} = 10 \text{ kHz}$, $L = 5 \text{ mH}$, $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$, compute: (i) the output voltage, (ii) the average and RMS currents through the switch, and (iii) the average current through the freewheeling diode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Working of Buck Converter in CCM:**

A buck converter consists of a **DC voltage source** $V_{s}$, a **controlled switch** $S$ (MOSFET/IGBT), a **freewheeling diode** $D_{f}$, a **filter inductor** $L$, a **filter capacitor** $C$, and a **load resistor** $R$, as shown in the circuit diagram in Section 4.1. The switch is gated by a PWM signal of frequency $f_{s}$ and duty cycle $D$.

**Mode 1: Switch ON ($0 \lt t \lt D T_{s}$)**

The switch is closed, connecting the source to the load via the inductor. The diode is reverse-biased and OFF. The current path is: $V_{s} \to S \to L \to C \parallel R \to \text{ground} \to V_{s}$. The voltage across the inductor is positive:

$$v_{L} = V_{s} - V_{o} \gt 0$$

Hence the inductor current **ramps up linearly**, storing energy in its magnetic field. The capacitor charges toward $V_{o}$.

**Mode 2: Switch OFF ($D T_{s} \lt t \lt T_{s}$)**

The switch opens, disconnecting the source. The inductor opposes the change in current and reverses its polarity, forward-biasing the freewheeling diode. The current path is: $L \to C \parallel R \to \text{ground} \to D_{f} \to L$. The voltage across the inductor is negative:

$$v_{L} = -V_{o} \lt 0$$

Hence the inductor current **ramps down linearly**, releasing its stored energy to the load. The capacitor discharges to maintain $V_{o}$.

**Derivation of Output Voltage:**

Applying volt-second balance (as derived in Section 3.1):

$$(V_{s} - V_{o}) D T_{s} + (-V_{o}) (1 - D) T_{s} = 0$$

Solving yields:

$$\boxed{V_{o} = D \cdot V_{s}}$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Computations:**

**Given:** $V_{s} = 100 \text{ V}$, $D = 0.6$, $f_{s} = 10 \text{ kHz}$, $L = 5 \text{ mH}$, $R = 20 \text{ }\Omega$.

**(i) Output Voltage:**

$$V_{o} = D \cdot V_{s} = 0.6 \times 100 = 60 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Average and RMS Switch Currents:**

Average load current = average inductor current (in CCM):

$$I_{o} = I_{L,avg} = \dfrac{V_{o}}{R} = \dfrac{60}{20} = 3 \text{ A}$$

Average switch current (the switch conducts only during the ON-time, carrying $I_{L,avg}$):

$$I_{S,avg} = D \cdot I_{L,avg} = 0.6 \times 3 = 1.8 \text{ A}$$

RMS switch current (assuming triangular ripple, but using the standard textbook approximation):

$$I_{S,rms} = I_{L,avg} \sqrt{D} = 3 \times \sqrt{0.6} = 3 \times 0.7746 = 2.324 \text{ A}$$

**(iii) Average Diode Current:**

The diode conducts only during the OFF-time, carrying $I_{L,avg}$:

$$I_{D,avg} = (1 - D) \cdot I_{L,avg} = (1 - 0.6) \times 3 = 0.4 \times 3 = 1.2 \text{ A}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical):**
> 1. **The circuit diagram is worth 2 marks.** If you skip it, you lose those marks. Draw it neatly with all five components labeled.
> 2. **The "current path" arrows** must be shown in both modes. The examiner specifically checks that students understand the freewheeling action of the diode — this is the most commonly misunderstood part.
> 3. **For RMS switch current**, students often confuse the formula. The correct one is $I_{S,rms} = I_{o} \sqrt{D}$, **not** $I_{S,rms} = I_{o} \cdot D$. Using the wrong formula can cost 1-2 marks.
> 4. **The diode and switch current averages** must sum to $I_{L,avg}$: verify $1.8 + 1.2 = 3.0$ ✓. This is a quick sanity check the examiner will look for.

---

### 5.3 KTU 2024 Scheme — Common Question Variants to Prepare

Based on past KTU papers, the buck converter is typically tested in the following ways:

| Question Type | Typical Sub-parts | Marks |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Define buck converter, state $V_{o}/V_{s}$ ratio | (a) Definition (b) Derivation | 3 + 7 = 10 |
| Compute $V_{o}$, $I_{o}$, $\Delta I_{L}$ from given data | (a) $V_{o}$ (b) Current quantities | 7 + 7 = 14 |
| Verify CCM/DCM and design $L$ or $C$ | (a) Ripple (b) Boundary condition | 7 + 7 = 14 |
| Comparison with boost converter | (a) Buck (b) Boost | 7 + 7 = 14 |
| Draw waveforms for $v_{L}$, $i_{L}$, $V_{o}$ | (a) Circuit + Modes (b) Waveforms + Calculation | 7 + 7 = 14 |

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!NOTE]
> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently tick every box below:**

- [ ] Define a buck converter as a **step-down** DC-DC chopper and write $V_{o} = D V_{s}$.
- [ ] Draw the **circuit diagram** with all five components labeled: source, switch, diode, inductor, capacitor, load.
- [ ] Identify the **direction of the freewheeling diode** (anode at ground, cathode at the inductor-load junction).
- [ ] State and apply the **Volt-Second Balance** theorem to derive $V_{o}/V_{s} = D$.
- [ ] Compute **average inductor current** as $I_{L,avg} = V_{o}/R$ in CCM.
- [ ] Compute **inductor current ripple** as $\Delta I_{L} = V_{o}(1-D) / (L f_{s})$.
- [ ] Compute **output voltage ripple** as $\Delta V_{o} = V_{o}(1-D) / (8 L C f_{s}^{2})$.
- [ ] Compute **minimum inductance for CCM**: $L_{min} = (1-D) R / (2 f_{s})$.
- [ ] Compute **critical load resistance**: $R_{crit} = 2 f_{s} L / (1-D)$.
- [ ] Compute **average switch current**: $I_{S,avg} = D I_{o}$.
- [ ] Compute **RMS switch current**: $I_{S,rms} = I_{o} \sqrt{D}$.
- [ ] Compute **average diode current**: $I_{D,avg} = (1-D) I_{o}$.
- [ ] Sketch the **waveforms** of $v_{L}(t)$, $i_{L}(t)$, $V_{o}(t)$, and the switch-gating signal.
- [ ] Distinguish **CCM vs DCM** by the sign of $I_{L,min}$.
- [ ] State the **decision rule**: $L \gt L_{min}$ → CCM; $L \lt L_{min}$ → DCM.
- [ ] List **three real-world applications** (laptop charger, LED driver, EV auxiliary).
- [ ] Remember: **Inductor current is always continuous** in a buck converter (the inductor is in series with the load), even though the switch current is discontinuous.
- [ ] **Units check:** $\Delta I_{L}$ in A, $\Delta V_{o}$ in V, $L$ in H, $C$ in F, $f_{s}$ in Hz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Mantra for Buck Converter:**
> *"Buck is step-down. $V_{o}$ equals $D$ times $V_{s}$. Inductor ripple depends on $V_{o}$, $1$ minus $D$, $L$, and $f_{s}$. For CCM, the inductor must be larger than $L_{min}$. When in doubt, draw the circuit, label the two modes, and apply volt-second balance. Full marks guaranteed."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Boost and Buck-Boost DC-DC Converters. Waveforms and Expression of DC-DC Converters for Output Voltage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT613 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boost and Buck-Boost DC-DC Converters. Waveforms and Expression of DC-DC Converters for Output Voltage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Boost Converter (Step-Up Chopper)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Boost Converter** is a switch-mode DC-DC power electronic converter whose average output DC voltage ($V_o$) is **greater than** the input DC source voltage ($V_s$). It operates by storing energy in an inductor during the switch ON-time and transferring that *stored* energy plus the source energy to the load during the switch OFF-time. The output is non-isolated and shares a common ground with the input.

**Real-World Analogy — The "Water Pump with a Spring Tank":**
Imagine you have a low-pressure water tap (the source $V_s$) and you want to fill a high-pressure overhead tank (the load at $V_o$). You cannot connect them directly. Instead, you install a pump that:
1. **Pump stroke (Switch ON):** Water from the tap is pumped into a sealed spring-loaded accumulator. The spring gets compressed (energy stored in the inductor $L$).
2. **Valve release (Switch OFF):** The pump stops, a valve opens, and *both* the tap water **and** the compressed spring push water *together* into the overhead tank, achieving a pressure higher than the tap alone could provide.

This is exactly what the boost converter does with electrons — it uses an inductor as the "spring" to step up the voltage.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** The boost converter can only **step up** ($V_o > V_s$). It cannot step down. The output voltage polarity is the **same** as the input polarity (non-inverting).

---

## 1.2 The Buck-Boost Converter (Step-Up/Down or Inverting Chopper)

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** A **Buck-Boost Converter** is a switch-mode DC-DC converter whose output voltage magnitude can be either **greater than or less than** the input voltage, depending on the duty cycle $D$. It is the cascade (topological dual) of a buck and a boost stage sharing a common inductor. The output is **inverted** with respect to the input, i.e., $V_o$ is of **opposite polarity** to $V_s$.

**Real-World Analogy — The "Bucket-Brigade with a Spring":**
Imagine carrying water from a low well to a high roof tank, but you have only one bucket. You cannot push water uphill continuously. So you:
1. **Fill the bucket (Switch ON):** Lower a bucket on a spring into the well, fill it (inductor stores energy, diode is reverse-biased, load is fed by capacitor only).
2. **Lift the bucket (Switch OFF):** Lift the spring-loaded bucket *above* the source level and pour it into the roof tank. The spring helps, so the bucket reaches a height that can be **above or below** the source height depending on how hard you push (duty cycle). The water poured into the roof tank flows *downward* from the bucket, so the tank's water level reads "negative" relative to the well — this is the **polarity inversion**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property:** The buck-boost converter can step **up or down**, and the output is **inverted**. In KTU problems, students very often lose marks by forgetting the negative sign in the output equation.

---

## 1.3 Assumptions Used in KTU-Level Analysis (Continuous Conduction Mode – CCM)

For both converters, the standard KTU derivation assumes:

- All components are **ideal** (lossless switch, diode, inductor, capacitor).
- The output capacitor $C$ is **large** so that output voltage $V_o$ is **constant and ripple-free**.
- The inductor current $i_L$ is **continuous** (CCM).
- The converter operates in **steady state** (periodic steady state — average inductor voltage and average capacitor current over one switching period $T_s$ are zero).
- Switching frequency $f_s = 1/T_s$ is constant; duty cycle $D = t_{on}/T_s$.

---

## 1.4 Circuit Topology — Schematic Text Description

> **Boost Converter Circuit:** Input DC source $V_s$ in series with inductor $L$ in series with the parallel combination of (a) switch $S$ (typically a MOSFET/IGBT) to ground and (b) diode $D$ in series with the parallel combination of capacitor $C$ and load resistor $R$. The diode's cathode connects to the $V_o$ positive terminal.

> **Buck-Boost Converter Circuit:** Input DC source $V_s$ in series with inductor $L$ in series with the parallel combination of (a) switch $S$ to ground and (b) diode $D$ whose cathode connects to the negative terminal of the parallel combination of capacitor $C$ and load $R$. The load's positive terminal is grounded; the negative terminal is the output node, giving **inverted polarity**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Boost Converter — Operating Principle (Step by Step)

The boost converter has **two switching states** per period $T_s$ in CCM:

### State 1: Switch $S$ is **ON** (Interval $DT_s$, $0 \le t \le DT_s$)

- The diode $D$ is **reverse-biased** and is OFF (it blocks because its cathode is at $V_o > V_s$ and its anode is at the switched node which is at $\approx 0$ V).
- The inductor $L$ is connected directly across the source $V_s$.
- Inductor current $i_L$ **ramps up linearly**; energy $E = \frac{1}{2} L i_L^2$ is stored.
- The capacitor $C$ (pre-charged to $V_o$) supplies the load current $I_o$ alone; $V_o$ droops slightly.
- Inductor voltage: $v_L = V_s$
- Inductor current slope: $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = \dfrac{V_s}{L}$

### State 2: Switch $S$ is **OFF** (Interval $(1-D)T_s$, $DT_s \le t \le T_s$)

- The diode $D$ becomes **forward-biased** and conducts.
- The inductor $L$ is now in series with the source $V_s$, and this series combination is connected across the load ($V_o$).
- Because $V_o > V_s$ (in steady state), the inductor sees a **negative** voltage $V_s - V_o$ and its current **ramps down linearly**, releasing its stored energy into the capacitor and load.
- Capacitor $C$ recharges to $V_o$.
- Inductor voltage: $v_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative)
- Inductor current slope: $\dfrac{di_L}{dt} = \dfrac{V_s - V_o}{L}$ (negative)

### Steady-State Condition: Volt-Second Balance on the Inductor

In periodic steady state, the **average voltage across the inductor over one full period must be zero** (otherwise $i_L$ would ramp up indefinitely and saturate the inductor). Applying this to the boost converter:

$$\int_0^{T_s} v_L \, dt = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (V_s)(DT_s) + (V_s - V_o)\big[(1-D)T_s\big] = 0$$

This is the **founding equation** of the boost converter, from which $V_o$ is derived in SECTION 3.

### Output Voltage Polarity and Magnitude

- **Polarity:** $V_o$ is **positive**, same polarity as $V_s$ (non-inverting).
- **Magnitude:** $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$, which is always $\ge V_s$ because $0 \le D < 1$.

### Boundary Between CCM and DCM

The boost converter enters DCM when the inductor current just touches zero at the end of the OFF interval. The critical load current is:

$$I_{o,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{V_s \, D(1-D)^2}{2 f_s L}$$

---

## 2.2 Buck-Boost Converter — Operating Principle (Step by Step)

### State 1: Switch $S$ is **ON** (Interval $DT_s$)

- The diode $D$ is **reverse-biased** (OFF) because its cathode is at the load (negative $V_o$ node) and its anode is at ground/positive node — actually, careful analysis shows the diode is reverse-biased because the switch shorts the diode's anode to ground potential while the cathode is at $-V_o$ which is below ground.
- The inductor $L$ is connected directly across $V_s$.
- Inductor current $i_L$ ramps **up** linearly.
- Capacitor $C$ (charged to $-V_o$ relative to ground) supplies the load alone.
- $v_L = V_s$, slope = $V_s/L$.

### State 2: Switch $S$ is **OFF** (Interval $(1-D)T_s$)

- The diode $D$ is **forward-biased** (ON) and provides a path for the inductor current.
- The inductor $L$ is now connected across the load (the capacitor and resistor), so $v_L = V_o$ (note: $V_o$ is negative, so the inductor sees a voltage that drives its current down).
- Inductor current ramps **down** linearly; energy is delivered to the load with **reversed polarity**.
- $v_L = V_o$ (negative value), slope = $V_o/L$ (negative).

### Steady-State Condition: Volt-Second Balance

$$(V_s)(DT_s) + (V_o)\big[(1-D)T_s\big] = 0$$

Solving gives the famous inverting transfer function.

### Output Voltage Polarity and Magnitude

- **Polarity:** $V_o$ is **negative** with respect to the grounded input (inverting).
- **Magnitude:** $\vert V_o \vert = V_s \dfrac{D}{1-D}$, which is $> V_s$ for $D > 0.5$ and $< V_s$ for $D < 0.5$.

### Critical (Boundary) Load Current

$$I_{o,\text{crit}} = \dfrac{V_s \, D(1-D)}{2 f_s L}$$

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mastery Table — Print this and keep it for last-minute revision. All symbols use $V$ = volts, $A$ = amps, $H$ = henries, $F$ = farads, $Hz$ = hertz, $s$ = seconds.**

| # | Quantity | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Switch ON interval | $t_{on} = D T_s$ | $t_{on} = D T_s$ |
| 2 | Switch OFF interval | $t_{off} = (1-D) T_s$ | $t_{off} = (1-D) T_s$ |
| 3 | Inductor voltage, ON state | $v_L = V_s$ | $v_L = V_s$ |
| 4 | Inductor voltage, OFF state | $v_L = V_s - V_o$ | $v_L = V_o$ (negative) |
| 5 | Inductor peak-to-peak ripple | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \, D}{f_s L}$ | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s \, D}{f_s L}$ |
| 6 | **Output voltage $V_o$** | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $-\dfrac{V_s \, D}{1-D}$ |
| 7 | Output polarity vs input | **Same** (non-inverting) | **Opposite** (inverting) |
| 8 | Average input current $I_s$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o \, D}{1-D}$ |
| 9 | Average inductor current $I_L$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ | $\dfrac{I_o}{1-D}$ |
| 10 | Average diode current $I_D$ | $I_o$ | $I_o$ |
| 11 | RMS switch current $I_{S,\text{rms}}$ | $I_o \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{(1-D)^2} + \dfrac{D}{3}\left(\dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2I_o/(1-D)}\right)^2}$ | Complex; consult reference |
| 12 | Critical load current $I_{oB}$ (CCM/DCM boundary) | $\dfrac{V_s \, D(1-D)^2}{2 f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{V_s \, D(1-D)}{2 f_s L}$ |
| 13 | Output voltage ripple $\Delta V_o / V_o$ | $\dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ | $\dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$ |
| 14 | Switch voltage stress $V_{S,\text{max}}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D} = V_o$ | $V_s + \vert V_o \vert = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ |
| 15 | Diode voltage stress $V_{D,\text{max}}$ | $\dfrac{V_s}{1-D} = V_o$ | $V_s + \vert V_o \vert = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Quick-Recall Mnemonic:** *Boost* = "divide by $(1-D)$", *Buck-Boost* = "negative, times $D/(1-D)$". In the KTU exam, the most common mistake is writing $V_o = V_s/(1-D)$ for the **buck-boost** as well — **don't do that!**

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boost converters** are ubiquitous in **battery-powered systems** (laptops, EVs, photovoltaic MPPT) where a low battery voltage (e.g., 12 V) must be raised to a higher bus voltage (e.g., 48 V or 400 V). They are the workhorse of **solar PV micro-inverters** and **LED drivers**.
- **Buck-boost converters** are used where the input voltage can be **above or below** the desired output — for example, in **automotive systems** (battery voltage 9 V–16 V must power a 12 V regulated load), in **battery-discharge regulators**, and in **portable devices** powered by a single Li-ion cell whose voltage (3.0 V–4.2 V) must drive a 3.3 V rail.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Boost Converter Output Voltage $V_o$

We apply the **volt-second balance** (also called inductor volt-second equilibrium) to inductor $L$ in steady state.

### Step 1: Inductor voltage during ON state

When the switch is ON (duration $D T_s$), the diode is OFF, and the full source voltage appears across the inductor:

$$v_{L,\text{ON}} = V_s$$

The current rises linearly. The change in inductor current during ON time is:

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{ON}} = \dfrac{V_s}{L} \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L}$$

### Step 2: Inductor voltage during OFF state

When the switch is OFF (duration $(1-D)T_s$), the diode conducts, and the inductor sees the difference between source and output:

$$v_{L,\text{OFF}} = V_s - V_o$$

Because $V_o > V_s$ in steady state, this voltage is **negative**, and the inductor current falls linearly. The change in current during OFF time is:

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{OFF}} = \dfrac{V_s - V_o}{L} \cdot (1-D)T_s = \dfrac{(V_s - V_o)(1-D)}{f_s L}$$

### Step 3: Steady-state condition

In periodic steady state, the net change in inductor current over one full period must be zero (otherwise flux would build up and saturate the core). Therefore the rise during ON must equal the fall during OFF in magnitude:

$$\Delta I_{L,\text{ON}} = -\Delta I_{L,\text{OFF}}$$

Substituting:

$$\dfrac{V_s \, D}{f_s L} = -\dfrac{(V_s - V_o)(1-D)}{f_s L}$$

The $f_s L$ cancels:

$$V_s \, D = -(V_s - V_o)(1-D)$$

### Step 4: Algebraic solution for $V_o$

Expand the right-hand side:

$$V_s \, D = -(V_s)(1-D) + V_o(1-D)$$

Bring $V_s$ terms to the left:

$$V_s \, D + V_s(1-D) = V_o(1-D)$$

Factor $V_s$ on the left:

$$V_s \big[D + (1-D)\big] = V_o(1-D)$$

The bracket simplifies to $1$:

$$V_s \cdot 1 = V_o(1-D)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}\,}$$

This is the **boost converter transfer function** in CCM. Since $0 \le D < 1$, the denominator is in $(0,1]$, hence $V_o \ge V_s$ always.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Buck-Boost Converter Output Voltage $V_o$

### Step 1: Inductor voltage during ON state

Same as boost — the inductor is across $V_s$:

$$v_{L,\text{ON}} = V_s \quad \text{for interval } D T_s$$

### Step 2: Inductor voltage during OFF state

When the switch is OFF, the inductor current freewheels through the diode and into the load/capacitor. The inductor voltage equals the **output voltage** (which is negative):

$$v_{L,\text{OFF}} = V_o \quad \text{(a negative quantity)} \quad \text{for interval } (1-D)T_s$$

### Step 3: Volt-second balance

$$\int_0^{T_s} v_L \, dt = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_s \cdot D T_s + V_o \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve for $V_o$

Divide by $T_s$:

$$V_s \, D + V_o (1-D) = 0$$

Isolate $V_o$:

$$V_o (1-D) = -V_s D$$

$$\boxed{\,V_o = -\dfrac{V_s \, D}{1-D}\,}$$

The **negative sign** is the defining feature of the buck-boost. The magnitude is $\vert V_o \vert = \dfrac{V_s D}{1-D}$:

- For $D < 0.5$: $\vert V_o \vert < V_s$ (buck mode, step-down).
- For $D = 0.5$: $\vert V_o \vert = V_s$ (unity gain).
- For $D > 0.5$: $\vert V_o \vert > V_s$ (boost mode, step-up).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Inductor Current Ripple $\Delta I_L$ (Boost)

We use the ON-state result from §3.1. The current ripple is the peak-to-peak swing:

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s}{L} \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{V_s D}{L f_s} = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L}$$

This is the most commonly required sub-result in KTU numerical problems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Some textbooks write $\Delta I_L = V_o D / (f_s L)$ for the boost converter. **This is wrong in terms of $V_s$** but the *numerical* value is the same *only if* $V_s = V_o(1-D)$, which is the steady-state relation. Stick to the $V_s$ form during derivation for clarity, then substitute $V_o$ at the end.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical (Boundary) Load Current for Boost Converter

The boundary between CCM and DCM occurs when the inductor current just reaches zero at the end of the OFF interval. The average inductor current at the boundary is half the peak-to-peak ripple:

$$I_{L,B} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \dfrac{V_s D}{2 f_s L}$$

The output current is related to the inductor current by charge balance on the capacitor: only the diode delivers charge to the load, and the diode conducts for $(1-D)T_s$, so:

$$I_{o,B} = I_{L,B} \cdot (1-D) = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{2 f_s L} \cdot (1-D) = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)^2}{2 f_s L}$$

For operation in CCM, the load current must exceed this value:

$$I_o \ge I_{o,B} \quad \text{for CCM}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Critical (Boundary) Load Current for Buck-Boost Converter

By an analogous procedure, applying volt-second balance and amp-second balance:

$$I_{L,B} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = \dfrac{V_s D}{2 f_s L}$$

The diode conducts for $(1-D)T_s$ and delivers the entire load current on average, giving:

$$I_{o,B} = I_{L,B} \cdot (1-D) = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{2 f_s L}$$

---

## 3.6 Derivation 6 — Output Voltage Ripple $\Delta V_o$

The output capacitor $C$ supplies the load current $I_o$ during the ON interval of duration $D T_s$ (when the switch is shorting, the diode is OFF, and the load is fed **only** by the capacitor). The charge removed from the capacitor during this interval is:

$$\Delta Q = I_o \cdot D T_s = \dfrac{I_o D}{f_s}$$

The corresponding voltage droop is:

$$\Delta V_o = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{C} = \dfrac{I_o D}{f_s C}$$

Dividing by $V_o$ gives the **relative ripple** often asked in KTU problems:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{D}{R C f_s}$$

where $R = V_o / I_o$ is the load resistance.

This result holds for **both** boost and buck-boost converters because in both cases the capacitor is the only source of load current during the switch ON interval.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Summary

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these distinctions matter in the exam:** KTU frequently gives a numerical problem like *"A boost converter has $V_s = 12$ V, $D = 0.4$, $L = 100\,\mu$H, $f_s = 25$ kHz, $R = 20\,\Omega$. Find $V_o$, $\Delta I_L$, $I_L$, $I_{oB}$, and check the conduction mode."* You must:
> 1. Compute $V_o$ using the right transfer function.
> 2. Compute $\Delta I_L$ using the right ON-state voltage.
> 3. Compute $I_L = I_o/(1-D)$ (or its buck-boost equivalent).
> 4. Compute $I_{oB}$ and compare with $I_o = V_o/R$.
> 5. State the mode.

The exact same workflow applies to the buck-boost, with the polarity and the (1-D) factor adjusted.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boost Converter — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["DC Source Vs"] --> ind["Inductor L"]
    ind --> nodeX["Switching Node Nx"]
    nodeX --> sw["Switch S MOSFET"]
    sw --> gndA["Ground GND"]
    nodeX --> dio["Diode D Anode"]
    dio --> cap["Capacitor C"]
    cap --> loadR["Load Resistor R"]
    loadR --> gndB["Ground GND"]
    cap --- outPos["Output Positive Vo"]
    cap --- outNeg["Output Negative 0V GND"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The current path during **switch ON** is $V_s \to L \to S \to$ ground (loop 1, inductor charges). During **switch OFF** the path is $V_s \to L \to D \to C \parallel R$ (loop 2, inductor discharges into the load with $V_o$ polarity matching $V_s$).

---

## 4.2 Buck-Boost Converter — Block-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["DC Source Vs Positive"] --> ind["Inductor L"]
    ind --> nodeX["Switching Node Nx"]
    nodeX --> sw["Switch S MOSFET"]
    sw --> gndA["Ground GND"]
    nodeX --> dio["Diode D Cathode Side"]
    dio --> cap["Capacitor C and Load R"]
    cap --> outNeg["Output Negative Terminal Vo"]
    outNeg --- gndB["Ground GND is the positive output terminal"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The inductor is **in series with the source on the input side**, and the **load is connected between the diode cathode and ground** (which is also the source positive terminal — this is the inversion mechanism). When $S$ is ON, the inductor charges from $V_s$ and the capacitor alone feeds the load. When $S$ is OFF, the inductor's stored energy freewheels through the diode into the load with reversed polarity.

---

## 4.3 Boost Converter — Switching State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> StateON
    StateON : State 1 Switch ON duration DT
    StateON : Diode reverse biased OFF
    StateON : Inductor charges slope Vs over L
    StateON : Capacitor supplies load
    StateON : iL ramps up
    StateON --> StateOFF : at t equals DT
    StateOFF : State 2 Switch OFF duration 1 minus D times T
    StateOFF : Diode forward biased ON
    StateOFF : Inductor sees Vs minus Vo
    StateOFF : iL ramps down
    StateOFF : Capacitor recharges to Vo
    StateOFF --> StateON : at t equals T
    StateON : vL equals Vs
    StateOFF : vL equals Vs minus Vo
```

---

## 4.4 Buck-Boost Converter — Switching State Machine (Mermaid)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> PhaseA
    PhaseA : Phase A Switch ON duration DT
    PhaseA : Diode reverse biased OFF
    PhaseA : Inductor charges from Vs
    PhaseA : vL equals Vs positive
    PhaseA : iL ramps up
    PhaseA --> PhaseB : at t equals DT
    PhaseB : Phase B Switch OFF duration 1 minus D times T
    PhaseB : Diode forward biased ON
    PhaseB : Inductor freewheels into load
    PhaseB : vL equals Vo negative
    PhaseB : iL ramps down
    PhaseB : Capacitor charges to negative Vo
    PhaseB --> PhaseA : at t equals T
```

---

## 4.5 Boost Converter — Steady-State Waveform Map (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Boost Converter Steady State Waveforms over one period T
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Gate Signal
    Switch ON high    :a1, 0, 40
    Switch OFF low    :a2, 40, 100
    section Inductor Voltage vL
    Positive Vs       :b1, 0, 40
    Negative Vs minus Vo :b2, 40, 100
    section Inductor Current iL
    Ramping Up        :c1, 0, 40
    Ramping Down      :c2, 40, 100
    section Diode State
    Reverse Biased OFF :d1, 0, 40
    Forward Biased ON  :d2, 40, 100
    section Output Voltage
    Constant Vo       :crit, 0, 100
```

> **Interpretation:** During the first 40% of $T_s$ (i.e., $D=0.4$), the switch is ON, $v_L = +V_s$ and $i_L$ rises. During the remaining 60%, the switch is OFF, $v_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative) and $i_L$ falls. The output $V_o$ is held constant by the capacitor.

---

## 4.6 Buck-Boost Converter — Steady-State Waveform Map (Mermaid Timeline)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Buck Boost Converter Steady State Waveforms over one period T
    dateFormat X
    axisFormat %s
    section Gate Signal
    Switch ON high      :a1, 0, 40
    Switch OFF low      :a2, 40, 100
    section Inductor Voltage vL
    Positive Vs         :b1, 0, 40
    Negative Vo         :b2, 40, 100
    section Inductor Current iL
    Ramping Up          :c1, 0, 40
    Ramping Down        :c2, 40, 100
    section Diode State
    Reverse Biased OFF  :d1, 0, 40
    Forward Biased ON   :d2, 40, 100
    section Output Vo
    Constant Negative Vo :crit, 0, 100
```

> **Interpretation:** Identical structure to the boost, but the OFF-state inductor voltage equals $V_o$ (which is **negative**), and the output is **negative** with respect to ground.
