# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]

> **"With a neat circuit diagram, explain the working of a boost converter in continuous conduction mode. Derive the expression for its output voltage."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A boost converter is a step-up DC-DC converter consisting of an input DC source $V_s$, an inductor $L$, a controlled switch $S$ (MOSFET/IGBT), a diode $D$, a filter capacitor $C$, and a load resistor $R$, all connected as: $V_s$ in series with $L$, then the switch $S$ to ground, with the diode $D$ in series from the $L$-$S$ junction to the $C \parallel R$ output.

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le D T_s$):** Diode $D$ is reverse-biased and OFF. The inductor is connected across $V_s$, so $v_L = V_s$ and the inductor current rises linearly with slope $V_s/L$. The capacitor supplies the load.

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $D T_s \le t \le T_s$):** Diode $D$ conducts. The inductor is now in series with $V_s$ and connected across the load, so $v_L = V_s - V_o$ (a negative value), and the inductor current falls linearly. The capacitor recharges to $V_o$.

Applying **volt-second balance** on the inductor over one switching period:

$$V_s \cdot D T_s + (V_s - V_o) \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

Solving:

$$V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$$

Since $0 \le D < 1$, $V_o \ge V_s$, hence the converter is "step-up". **(3 marks — 1 for circuit, 1 for modes, 1 for derivation.)**

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]

> **"Compare the boost and buck-boost converters in terms of output voltage expression, polarity, and the boundary condition for continuous conduction mode."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Boost Converter | Buck-Boost Converter |
|---|---|---|
| Output voltage | $V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D}$ | $V_o = -\dfrac{V_s D}{1-D}$ |
| Polarity | Non-inverting (same as $V_s$) | Inverting (opposite to $V_s$) |
| Step-up/down | Step-up only | Step-up or step-down |
| Critical load current $I_{oB}$ (CCM/DCM boundary) | $\dfrac{V_s D (1-D)^2}{2 f_s L}$ | $\dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{2 f_s L}$ |

The boost always produces $V_o \ge V_s$ and cannot step down, while the buck-boost produces $\vert V_o \vert = V_s D/(1-D)$ which can be greater or smaller than $V_s$ depending on $D$, and the output is always of opposite polarity to the input. **(3 marks.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the buck-boost output equation — this costs 2 to 3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Mixing up the OFF-state inductor voltage** — for boost it is $V_s - V_o$, for buck-boost it is $V_o$. Getting this wrong invalidates the entire volt-second balance.
> 3. **Not stating the mode assumption** (CCM vs DCM) before solving — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark if the assumption is not made explicit.
> 4. **Confusing $I_L$ with $I_o$** — these are equal only in a buck converter. In boost/buck-boost, $I_L = I_o/(1-D)$.
> 5. **Using $V_o$ instead of $V_s$** in the $\Delta I_L$ formula during the *derivation* phase (acceptable to substitute at the end, but the form using $V_s$ scores better).
> 6. **Skipping the steady-state justification** (volt-second balance / inductor volt-second equilibrium) — examiners want this named explicitly.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]

> **A boost converter has the following specifications:**
> $V_s = 24$ V, $D = 0.4$, switching frequency $f_s = 50$ kHz, $L = 200\,\mu$H, $C = 100\,\mu$F, load resistance $R = 20\,\Omega$.
>
> **(a)** Derive and calculate the output voltage $V_o$ and the average output current $I_o$. Verify the converter is operating in CCM. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Calculate the inductor current ripple $\Delta I_L$, the average inductor current $I_L$, the average source current $I_s$, and the output voltage ripple $\Delta V_o$. Sketch the steady-state waveforms of $v_{GS}$, $i_L$, and $v_o$. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Output Voltage and Mode Verification (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Output voltage:**
Using the boost transfer function derived via volt-second balance:

$$V_o = \dfrac{V_s}{1-D} = \dfrac{24}{1-0.4} = \dfrac{24}{0.6} = 40 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Output current:**
The load is resistive, so:

$$I_o = \dfrac{V_o}{R} = \dfrac{40}{20} = 2 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Inductor ripple:**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L} = \dfrac{24 \times 0.4}{50 \times 10^3 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{9.6}{10} = 0.96 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Average inductor current:**

$$I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D} = \dfrac{2}{0.6} = 3.333 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Critical (boundary) load current:**

$$I_{oB} = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)^2}{2 f_s L} = \dfrac{24 \times 0.4 \times (0.6)^2}{2 \times 50 \times 10^3 \times 200 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{24 \times 0.4 \times 0.36}{20} = \dfrac{3.456}{20} = 0.1728 \text{ A}$$

**Step 6 — Mode verification:**
Since $I_o = 2$ A $> I_{oB} = 0.1728$ A, the inductor current is **continuous** and the converter operates in **CCM**. ✅

*(7 marks — 2 for $V_o$, 1 for $I_o$, 2 for ripple and $I_L$, 1 for $I_{oB}$, 1 for mode statement.)*

#### Part (b) — Detailed Waveforms and Ripple Analysis (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Average source current:**
By input-output power balance (ideal converter, $P_s = P_o$):

$$I_s = \dfrac{V_o I_o}{V_s} = \dfrac{40 \times 2}{24} = 3.333 \text{ A}$$

Note: $I_s = I_L$ in the boost, consistent with our earlier result.

**Step 2 — Peak and minimum inductor currents:**

$$I_{L,\text{max}} = I_L + \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 3.333 + 0.48 = 3.813 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{L,\text{min}} = I_L - \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{2} = 3.333 - 0.48 = 2.853 \text{ A}$$

Both are positive, confirming CCM.

**Step 3 — Output voltage ripple:**

$$\Delta V_o = \dfrac{I_o D}{f_s C} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.4}{50 \times 10^3 \times 100 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{0.8}{5} = 0.16 \text{ V}$$

Relative ripple:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{V_o} = \dfrac{0.16}{40} = 0.004 = 0.4\%$$

**Step 4 — Switch and diode voltage stress:**

$$V_{S,\text{max}} = V_{D,\text{max}} = V_o = 40 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 — Waveform sketch (textual description for KTU answer sheet):**

The student should draw a single-period timing diagram with the following traces aligned to the switching period $T_s = 1/f_s = 20\,\mu$s:

- **$v_{GS}$ (gate drive):** A rectangular pulse, high (e.g., +15 V) from $t = 0$ to $t = 8\,\mu$s ($= D T_s$), low (0 V or $-5$ V) from $t = 8\,\mu$s to $t = 20\,\mu$s.
- **$i_L$ (inductor current):** A triangular waveform with mean value $3.33$ A, peak $3.81$ A, valley $2.85$ A, period $20\,\mu$s, rising during the gate-high interval and falling during the gate-low interval.
- **$v_o$ (output voltage):** A nearly flat DC line at 40 V with a small sawtooth ripple of peak-to-peak amplitude $0.16$ V, drooping during the switch ON interval (capacitor discharging into the load) and rising during the switch OFF interval (capacitor being recharged through the diode).

*(7 marks — 2 for source current, 2 for peak/valley and ripple, 1 for switch/diode stress, 2 for waveform sketch quality and labels.)*

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **A buck-boost converter operates in CCM with the following specifications:**
> $V_s = 15$ V, $D = 0.6$, $f_s = 25$ kHz, $L = 250\,\mu$H, $C = 220\,\mu$F, $R = 10\,\Omega$.
>
> **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the two operating modes of the buck-boost converter. Derive the expression for the output voltage and calculate its magnitude and polarity for the given data. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** Calculate the average inductor current, inductor current ripple, the critical value of load resistance for CCM/DCM boundary, and check the conduction mode. Also calculate the output voltage ripple. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Operating Modes and Output Voltage (7 marks)

**Circuit diagram (text description for KTU answer sheet):** Draw the source $V_s$ with its positive terminal connected to one end of inductor $L$. The other end of $L$ (the switching node) is connected to (i) the drain of switch $S$ (MOSFET), whose source goes to the negative terminal of $V_s$ (ground), and (ii) the anode of diode $D$. The cathode of $D$ connects to the negative terminal of the parallel $RC$ load, and the positive terminal of the $RC$ load connects to ground. The output $V_o$ is taken with positive reference at ground and negative reference at the diode cathode, giving an **inverted** polarity.

**Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le D T_s$):**
- Diode $D$ is reverse-biased (OFF) — the inductor current has no path through the diode.
- Inductor is connected across the source: $v_L = V_s = +15$ V.
- Inductor current rises linearly with slope $V_s/L = 15 / 250\times10^{-6} = 6 \times 10^4$ A/s.
- Capacitor $C$ (charged to $-V_o$) supplies the load alone.

**Mode 2 (Switch OFF, $D T_s \le t \le T_s$):**
- Diode $D$ is forward-biased (ON).
- Inductor current freewheels through $D$ into the load: $v_L = V_o$ (a negative value, since $V_o$ is inverted).
- Inductor current falls linearly; energy is delivered to the load with reversed polarity.
- Capacitor recharges to the (negative) output voltage.

**Derivation of $V_o$:** Apply volt-second balance on the inductor:

$$V_s \cdot D T_s + V_o \cdot (1-D) T_s = 0$$

$$V_o = -\dfrac{V_s D}{1-D} = -\dfrac{15 \times 0.6}{1 - 0.6} = -\dfrac{9}{0.4} = -22.5 \text{ V}$$

**Magnitude:** $\vert V_o \vert = 22.5$ V (step-up, since $D > 0.5$).
**Polarity:** **Negative** with respect to the grounded input positive terminal.

*(7 marks — 2 for circuit, 2 for modes, 2 for derivation, 1 for numerical result with polarity correctly stated.)*

#### Part (b) — Currents, Boundary, and Ripple (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Average output current:**

$$I_o = \dfrac{\vert V_o \vert}{R} = \dfrac{22.5}{10} = 2.25 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2 — Average inductor current:**

$$I_L = \dfrac{I_o}{1-D} = \dfrac{2.25}{0.4} = 5.625 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3 — Inductor current ripple:**

$$\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_s D}{f_s L} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.6}{25 \times 10^3 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{9}{6.25} = 1.44 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4 — Peak and minimum inductor currents:**

$$I_{L,\text{max}} = 5.625 + 0.72 = 6.345 \text{ A}, \quad I_{L,\text{min}} = 5.625 - 0.72 = 4.905 \text{ A}$$

Both positive, confirming the converter stays in CCM with this specific $L$.

**Step 5 — Critical load resistance for boundary:**
The boundary condition is $I_o = I_{oB}$:

$$I_{oB} = \dfrac{V_s D (1-D)}{2 f_s L} = \dfrac{15 \times 0.6 \times 0.4}{2 \times 25 \times 10^3 \times 250 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{3.6}{12.5} = 0.288 \text{ A}$$

The corresponding boundary load resistance is:

$$R_B = \dfrac{\vert V_o \vert}{I_{oB}} = \dfrac{V_s D / (1-D)}{I_{oB}} = \dfrac{22.5}{0.288} \approx 78.125 \, \Omega$$

**Step 6 — Mode check:**
Since the actual load resistance $R = 10\,\Omega \ll R_B = 78.1\,\Omega$ (equivalently, $I_o = 2.25$ A $\gg I_{oB} = 0.288$ A), the converter is in **CCM**. ✅

**Step 7 — Output voltage ripple:**

$$\Delta V_o = \dfrac{I_o D}{f_s C} = \dfrac{2.25 \times 0.6}{25 \times 10^3 \times 220 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{1.35}{5.5} = 0.2455 \text{ V}$$

Relative ripple:

$$\dfrac{\Delta V_o}{\vert V_o \vert} = \dfrac{0.2455}{22.5} \approx 1.09\%$$

**Step 8 — Switch and diode voltage stress:**

$$V_{S,\text{max}} = V_{D,\text{max}} = V_s + \vert V_o \vert = 15 + 22.5 = 37.5 \text{ V}$$

*(7 marks — 1 for $I_o$, 1 for $I_L$, 1 for $\Delta I_L$, 1 for $I_{L,\text{max/min}}$, 1 for boundary, 1 for mode check, 1 for ripple and stress.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boost and Buck-Boost DC-DC Converters**

- **Boost converter output:** $V_o = V_s / (1-D)$ — always $\ge V_s$, same polarity as $V_s$.
- **Buck-boost converter output:** $V_o = -V_s D / (1-D)$ — always opposite polarity; magnitude can be less than, equal to, or greater than $V_s$ depending on $D$.
- **Founding principle for both:** **Volt-second balance** on the inductor over one switching period in steady state.
- **Inductor ON-state voltage** in both converters: $v_L = V_s$ (positive).
- **Inductor OFF-state voltage:** boost → $v_L = V_s - V_o$ (negative); buck-boost → $v_L = V_o$ (negative).
- **Inductor current ripple:** $\Delta I_L = V_s D / (f_s L)$ for both converters.
- **Average inductor current:** $I_L = I_o / (1-D)$ for both converters (because charge balance on the capacitor means diode delivers $I_o$ for fraction $(1-D)$ of the period, with average diode current = $I_L(1-D)$).
- **Boundary condition for CCM:** $I_o \ge I_{oB}$, where $I_{oB,\text{boost}} = V_s D (1-D)^2 / (2 f_s L)$ and $I_{oB,\text{buck-boost}} = V_s D (1-D) / (2 f_s L)$.
- **Output voltage ripple:** $\Delta V_o = I_o D / (f_s C)$ for both converters (capacitor supplies the load alone during switch ON interval).
- **Switch and diode voltage stress:**
  - Boost: $V_{S,\text{max}} = V_{D,\text{max}} = V_o$.
  - Buck-boost: $V_{S,\text{max}} = V_{D,\text{max}} = V_s + \vert V_o \vert = V_s / (1-D)$.
- **Waveforms to always sketch** (in any 7-mark or 14-mark answer): gate drive $v_{GS}$, inductor current $i_L$ (triangular), inductor voltage $v_L$ (rectangular bipolar), diode current $i_D$ (pulsed), output voltage $v_o$ (DC with small ripple).
- **KTU exam traps to avoid:**
  - Sign error in buck-boost output.
  - Using the wrong OFF-state inductor voltage.
  - Confusing $I_L$ with $I_o$.
  - Forgetting to verify CCM/DCM before using the CCM transfer function.
  - Mixing up the boundary resistance vs. boundary current formulas between boost and buck-boost.
- **Real-world applications to mention in descriptive answers:**
  - Boost: PV MPPT, LED drivers, hybrid electric vehicle auxiliary supplies, PFC (power factor correction) front ends.
  - Buck-boost: battery-powered portable electronics, automotive voltage regulators, single-cell Li-ion to 3.3 V/5 V rails, voltage sag corrector systems.

---

**End of Module 3 Notes — Boost and Buck-Boost DC-DC Converters. Waveforms and Expression of DC-DC Converters for Output Voltage.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Voltage and Current Ripple under Continuous Conduction Mode. Isolated Converters: Flyback
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEVT613 - POWER ELECTRONICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Voltage and Current Ripple under Continuous Conduction Mode. Isolated Converters: Flyback

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Voltage and Current Ripple in Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** is the operating condition of a DC-DC switching converter in which the inductor current never falls to zero during a complete switching cycle. **Voltage and Current Ripple** refers to the small, periodic AC fluctuations superimposed on the average (DC) values of the output voltage and inductor/capacitor currents, caused by the charging and discharging of energy storage elements during the switch ON and OFF intervals.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, ripple analysis is an *outcome-based* skill mapped to **CO3** — *Analyse the operation of switched-mode power converters* — and is a guaranteed 7 to 14 mark question in every End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology**:
> - **$V_o$** → Average (DC) Output Voltage
> - **$\Delta V_o$** → Peak-to-Peak Output Voltage Ripple
> - **$\Delta I_L$** → Peak-to-Peak Inductor Current Ripple
> - **$D$** → Duty Cycle ($D = T_{on}/T_s$)
> - **$f_s = 1/T_s$** → Switching Frequency (typically **20 kHz to 500 kHz**)

---

### 1.2 Flyback Converter (Isolated Topology)

**Formal KTU Definition:**
> The **Flyback Converter** is an *isolated* switched-mode DC-DC power converter derived from the buck-boost topology by replacing the single inductor with a *mutually-coupled inductor* (a transformer with a gapped core). Energy is **stored in the magnetic field of the coupled inductor during the switch ON-time** and **transferred to the output during the switch OFF-time**. It provides both **galvanic isolation** and **step-up/step-down voltage conversion** in a single stage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Flyback = Buck-Boost + Isolation Transformer + Gapped Core**
> It is the *workhorse* of low-to-medium power isolated applications (**0 W to 150 W**), including mobile phone chargers, laptop adapters, TV standby supplies, and high-voltage DC supplies for CRTs and photocopiers.

---

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

**Analogy — A Spring-Loaded Water Pump:**

Imagine a **water pump system** where:
- A heavy spring is connected via a valve to a sealed water tank.
- During the **ON-time**, the spring is *compressed* (energy stored magnetically) and water is sucked in — but the output valve is **closed**, so no water leaves.
- During the **OFF-time**, the spring *releases* and pushes the water out through the output valve into the consumer tank.

In a **Flyback converter**:
- The **spring** = energy stored in the **gapped core of the coupled inductor** ($E = \frac{1}{2} L_p I_p^2$).
- The **input valve** = the **MOSFET switch**.
- The **output valve** = the **secondary-side diode**.
- The **isolated tank** = the **load**, separated by the transformer's dielectric barrier (galvanic isolation).

For **ripple**:
- Think of the **output capacitor as a buffer bucket** that fills when the spring releases and empties when the load draws current. Because the fill rate is *pulsed* (only during OFF-time), the water level (voltage) *oscillates slightly* around an average — this is exactly **voltage ripple**.

> [!TIP]
> The smaller the ripple, the larger the capacitor you need. But larger capacitors are expensive and bulky — this is the *classic engineering trade-off* KTU examiners love to test.

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Range / Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Switching Frequency $f_s$ | **20 kHz – 500 kHz** | Higher $f_s$ → smaller ripple, smaller magnetics |
| Duty Cycle $D$ | **0 < D < 1** | Control variable for output regulation |
| Coupling Coefficient $k$ | **0.95 – 0.99** (practical) | Ideal transformer assumed $k = 1$ |
| Turns Ratio $n = N_1/N_2$ | **0.1 – 10** | Sets voltage transfer ratio |
| Magnetizing Inductance $L_m$ | **10 µH – 1 mH** | Energy storage element |
| Allowed Output Voltage Ripple | **1% – 5%** of $V_o$ | Industrial design standard |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Voltage and Current Ripple Analysis in CCM

#### 2.1.1 Underlying Physical Principle

In a switching converter, two linear (slow) energy storage elements exist:
- **Inductor L** → smooths current
- **Capacitor C** → smooths voltage

The switching action creates a **pulsating energy transfer**, which causes small periodic deviations from the average. These deviations are **ripples**. In **CCM**, the inductor current ripple is *small* compared to its DC value ($I_L$), and we apply the **small-ripple approximation** (also called the *ripple approximation*).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Small-Ripple Approximation (KTU-favorite exam line):**
> $v_C(t) \approx V_C$ and $i_L(t) \approx I_L$ for the *purpose of calculating slopes* within one switching period. This is valid only when $\Delta V_o \ll V_o$ and $\Delta I_L \ll I_L$.

#### 2.1.2 Inductor Current Ripple — General Procedure

The voltage across an inductor is:
$$v_L(t) = L \frac{di_L(t)}{dt}$$

During the **ON-time** interval ($DT_s$), if the voltage across $L$ is $V_{L,\text{on}}$ (constant by small-ripple approximation), then the inductor current rises linearly:

$$\frac{\Delta i_L}{DT_s} = \frac{V_{L,\text{on}}}{L}$$

Similarly, during the **OFF-time** interval ($(1-D)T_s$), with $V_{L,\text{off}}$ across the inductor:

$$\frac{\Delta i_L}{(1-D)T_s} = -\frac{V_{L,\text{off}}}{L}$$

In **steady-state CCM**, the net change in inductor current over one full cycle must be zero (volt-second balance):

$$V_{L,\text{on}} \cdot DT_s = V_{L,\text{off}} \cdot (1-D)T_s$$

#### 2.1.3 Output Voltage Ripple — General Procedure

The capacitor's voltage is governed by:
$$i_C(t) = C \frac{dv_C(t)}{dt}$$

The output capacitor supplies the **AC component of the inductor current** (the DC component goes to the load). Therefore, the peak-to-peak voltage ripple is:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{1}{C} \int (\text{area of capacitor current waveform})$$

For a symmetric triangular ripple (typical in buck/boost):

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta I_L}{8 f_s C} \quad \text{(for symmetric triangular current)}}$$

Or in terms of the duty cycle and load:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2} \quad \text{(buck converter)}$$

---

### 2.2 Flyback Converter — Operation Principle

The Flyback converter consists of:
1. A **MOSFET/IGBT switch (S)** in series with the **primary winding** of a coupled inductor.
2. A **secondary winding** connected to a **rectifier diode (D)** and **output filter capacitor (C)**.
3. A **gapped ferrite core** (the air gap is *intentional* — it stores energy and prevents saturation).
4. **Turns ratio** $n = N_1/N_2$, with reflected load: $R' = n^2 R$.

#### 2.2.1 CCM Operation — Two Sub-Intervals

**Sub-Interval 1: Switch ON (0 < t < DT_s)**

- Switch S is **closed**; primary current $i_p$ ramps up linearly from $I_{p,\min}$ to $I_{p,\max}$.
- Primary voltage = $V_s$ (input). Reflected secondary voltage = $V_s/n$, which **reverse-biases** the output diode.
- Diode D is **OFF**; load is supplied entirely by the output capacitor C.
- Energy is **stored** in the magnetizing inductance $L_m$:

$$\frac{di_p}{dt} = \frac{V_s}{L_m} \implies i_p(t) = I_{p,\min} + \frac{V_s}{L_m} t$$

**Sub-Interval 2: Switch OFF (DT_s < t < T_s)**

- Switch S is **open**; the magnetizing current forces a polarity reversal (transformer dot convention).
- Secondary diode D becomes **forward-biased**; current $i_s$ flows to the load.
- Secondary current ramps down from $i_s(DT_s) = (I_{p,\max}/n)$ to $(I_{p,\min}/n)$.
- Energy is **released** to the output capacitor and load.

$$\frac{di_s}{dt} = -\frac{V_o}{L_m/n^2} = -\frac{n^2 V_o}{L_m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why an air gap?** In a Flyback transformer, the entire *magnetizing energy* must be stored in the core. Without a gap, the permeability is too high and saturation occurs at low current. The gap **lowers effective permeability** ($\mu_{\text{eff}}$), stores energy in the gap field, and **linearizes** the B-H curve, allowing higher stored energy without saturation.

#### 2.2.2 Voltage Transfer Ratio (CCM)

Applying volt-second balance to the magnetizing inductance (or the standard Flyback analysis):

$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{D}{n(1-D)}}$$

This is identical in *form* to the buck-boost converter, with the turns ratio $n$ providing an **additional design freedom**.

#### 2.2.3 Current Relationships

$$I_{p,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_o}{n} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D}$$

$$I_{s,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_o D}{1-D} \cdot \frac{1}{n} \cdot n = \frac{I_o D}{1-D} \quad \text{(verification: not used directly)}$$

$$I_{p,\text{peak}} = I_{p,\text{avg}} + \frac{\Delta i_p}{2}$$

$$\Delta i_p = \frac{V_s D T_s}{L_m} = \frac{V_s D}{L_m f_s}$$

#### 2.2.4 Output Voltage Ripple in Flyback

The output capacitor supplies the load during the **switch ON-time** (when the diode is reverse-biased). During this interval, the entire load current $I_o$ is drawn from C. Hence the ripple is:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{1}{C} \int_0^{DT_s} I_o \, dt = \frac{I_o D T_s}{C} = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s}$$

This is the **worst-case** ripple, since during OFF-time the diode supplies the load and recharges C.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quick Reference)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize this table completely** — these are the formulas KTU examiners set questions on.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Voltage Transfer Ratio (Buck) | $V_o/V_s = D$ | CCM, ideal switch & diode |
| 2 | Voltage Transfer Ratio (Boost) | $V_o/V_s = 1/(1-D)$ | CCM |
| 3 | Voltage Transfer Ratio (Buck-Boost) | $V_o/V_s = -D/(1-D)$ | CCM, polarity inverted |
| 4 | Voltage Transfer Ratio (Flyback) | $V_o/V_s = D/[n(1-D)]$ | CCM, isolated |
| 5 | Inductor Current Ripple (Buck) | $\Delta i_L = V_o(1-D)/(Lf_s)$ | CCM |
| 6 | Inductor Current Ripple (Boost) | $\Delta i_L = V_s D/(Lf_s)$ | CCM |
| 7 | Output Voltage Ripple (Buck) | $\Delta V_o = V_o(1-D)/(8LCf_s^2)$ | Symmetric triangular |
| 8 | Output Voltage Ripple (Boost) | $\Delta V_o = I_o D/(Cf_s)$ | Capacitor supplies load for $DT_s$ |
| 9 | Output Voltage Ripple (Flyback) | $\Delta V_o = I_o D/(Cf_s)$ | Same as boost — capacitor supplies load during ON-time |
| 10 | Boundary Conduction $K_{\text{crit}}$ | $K_{\text{crit}} = (1-D)^2$ (buck), etc. | For CCM/DCM boundary |
| 11 | Flyback Turns Ratio | $n = N_p/N_s = (V_s/V_o) \cdot [(1-D)/D]$ | From transfer ratio inverted |
| 12 | Primary Peak Current | $I_{p,\text{peak}} = I_o/[n(1-D)] + V_s D/(2 L_m f_s)$ | CCM |
| 13 | Magnetizing Energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} L_m I_{p,\text{peak}}^2$ | Stored in gapped core |
| 14 | Reflected Load | $R' = n^2 R$ | Seen at primary side |
| 15 | Switch Voltage Stress (Flyback) | $V_{sw,\text{max}} = V_s + (V_o \cdot N_p/N_s) = V_s + nV_o$ | Critical for MOSFET rating |
| 16 | Diode Voltage Stress (Flyback) | $V_{D,\text{max}} = V_o + (V_s/n)$ | Reverse recovery important |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake**: Sign of $V_o$ in buck-boost and Flyback. The output is **inverted** with respect to the input ground reference — students often forget this and lose 2 marks.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Telecom SMPS** — 48 V to 12 V/5 V isolated supplies in base stations (Flyback dominates < 100 W).
2. **Consumer Adapters** — Your laptop charger is a Flyback (rectified mains → isolated low-voltage DC).
3. **EV Auxiliary Supplies** — HV battery (400 V) to 12 V accessory bus via isolated Flyback.
4. **LED Drivers** — High-voltage, low-current isolated supplies.
5. **Medical Isolation** — Patient-connected devices require 4 kV galvanic isolation (Flyback with reinforced insulation).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Inductor Current Ripple in a Buck Converter (CCM)

> **Problem Statement:** A buck converter operates in CCM with input $V_s = 24$ V, output $V_o = 12$ V, switching frequency $f_s = 100$ kHz, and filter inductor $L = 100$ µH. Find the inductor current ripple $\Delta i_L$.

**Step 1: Identify the duty cycle (volt-second balance)**

For a buck converter in CCM:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_s} = D \implies D = \frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{12}{24} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Determine the voltage across L during the OFF-time**

During the OFF-time, the inductor is connected between the output (ground side via diode) and $V_o$. The voltage across L is:

$$V_{L,\text{off}} = V_o - V_s = 12 - 24 = -12 \text{ V}$$

But the *magnitude* of voltage across L during OFF-time is conventionally taken as $(V_s - V_o)$.

**Step 3: Apply the volt-second balance equation**

$$V_{L,\text{on}} \cdot DT_s = |V_{L,\text{off}}| \cdot (1-D)T_s$$

For a buck converter: $V_{L,\text{on}} = V_s - V_o$ and $|V_{L,\text{off}}| = V_o$. Therefore:

$$(V_s - V_o) D = V_o (1-D)$$

Solving:

$$V_s D = V_o D + V_o - V_o D \implies V_s D = V_o \implies D = \frac{V_o}{V_s} \checkmark$$

**Step 4: Compute the inductor current ripple**

The current ripple is determined by the voltage across the inductor during the ON-time:

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot D T_s}{L}$$

Substituting values:

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{(24 - 12) \times 0.5 \times (1/100{,}000)}{100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{12 \times 0.5 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{60 \times 10^{-6}}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.6 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta i_L = 0.6 \text{ A}}$$

**Step 5: Verification**

Check using OFF-time expression:

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{V_o (1-D) T_s}{L} = \frac{12 \times 0.5 \times 10\,\mu s}{100\,\mu H} = 0.6 \text{ A} \checkmark$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Output Voltage Ripple in a Flyback Converter (CCM)

> **Problem Statement:** A Flyback converter has $V_s = 48$ V, $V_o = 12$ V, $P_o = 60$ W, $n = N_p/N_s = 4$, $f_s = 50$ kHz, and output capacitor $C = 470$ µF. Find the peak-to-peak output voltage ripple.

**Step 1: Compute the duty cycle**

From the transfer ratio:

$$\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{D}{n(1-D)} \implies 12 = \frac{48 \cdot D}{4(1-D)}$$

$$12 = \frac{12 D}{1-D} \implies 12(1-D) = 12D \implies 12 - 12D = 12D \implies D = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Compute the average load current**

$$I_o = \frac{P_o}{V_o} = \frac{60}{12} = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Determine the capacitor current waveform**

In a Flyback, during the switch ON-time, the secondary diode is reverse-biased, and the **entire load current $I_o$ is supplied by the output capacitor C**. During the OFF-time, the diode conducts and *both* the load and the capacitor charging current flow.

Hence, the capacitor sees a **DC discharge of $I_o$ for duration $DT_s$** and a charge of similar magnitude during $(1-D)T_s$.

**Step 4: Compute the ripple**

The change in capacitor voltage during the discharge interval is:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{1}{C} \int_0^{DT_s} I_o \, dt = \frac{I_o \cdot DT_s}{C}$$

Substituting:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times (1/50{,}000)}{470 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{5 \times 0.5 \times 20 \times 10^{-6}}{470 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-6}}{470 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta V_o = 0.1064 \text{ V} \approx 106.4 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o = 106.4 \text{ mV} \quad \text{(which is } 0.89\% \text{ of } V_o)}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Flyback Switch Voltage Stress

> **Problem Statement:** For the same Flyback above, find the maximum voltage stress across the MOSFET switch.

**Step 1: Identify the voltage components**

When the MOSFET turns OFF, two voltages appear in series across it:
- The input voltage $V_s$ (because the primary is connected from $V_s$ to the drain).
- The reflected secondary voltage, which is $V_o \cdot (N_p/N_s) = V_o \cdot n$.

**Step 2: Apply KVL around the primary loop**

$$V_{ds} = V_s + n V_o$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$V_{ds,\text{max}} = 48 + (4)(12) = 48 + 48 = 96 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ds,\text{max}} = 96 \text{ V}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derating Note:** The MOSFET must be rated for **at least 1.5 × 96 = 144 V** to account for voltage spikes caused by transformer leakage inductance. A 150 V or 200 V MOSFET is typically chosen. This is a classic KTU *design consideration* question.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Output Voltage Ripple in a Boost Converter (CCM)

> **Problem Statement:** A boost converter has $V_s = 12$ V, $V_o = 24$ V, $I_o = 2$ A, $L = 200$ µH, $C = 220$ µF, $f_s = 50$ kHz. Find $\Delta V_o$.

**Step 1: Compute duty cycle**

$$D = 1 - \frac{V_s}{V_o} = 1 - \frac{12}{24} = 0.5$$

**Step 2: Identify the capacitor current waveform**

In a boost, the diode is ON for the OFF-time, conducting $i_L - I_o$ into the capacitor. During ON-time, the diode is OFF and the capacitor supplies the *entire* load current $I_o$. The ripple waveform is again a *piecewise linear* discharge followed by a partial recharge.

For a boost, the peak-to-peak ripple is:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{I_o D T_s}{C}$$

**Step 3: Substitute**

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{2 \times 0.5 \times (1/50{,}000)}{220 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{20 \times 10^{-6}}{220 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\Delta V_o = 0.0909 \text{ V} \approx 90.9 \text{ mV}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o \approx 90.9 \text{ mV} \quad (0.38\% \text{ of } V_o)}$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Flyback Magnetizing Inductance Design

> **Problem Statement:** For a Flyback with $V_s = 100$ V to $V_o = 5$ V, $P_o = 25$ W, $D = 0.4$, $f_s = 100$ kHz, $n = 5$. Find the minimum magnetizing inductance $L_m$ to maintain CCM with at least 25% ripple.

**Step 1: Compute average load current**

$$I_o = \frac{P_o}{V_o} = \frac{25}{5} = 5 \text{ A}$$

**Step 2: Compute average primary current**

$$I_{p,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_o}{n} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D} = \frac{5}{5} \cdot \frac{0.4}{0.6} = 0.667 \text{ A}$$

**Step 3: Apply 25% ripple criterion**

For CCM, we want $\Delta i_p \leq 0.25 \cdot I_{p,\text{avg}}$:

$$\Delta i_p = 0.25 \times 0.667 = 0.167 \text{ A}$$

**Step 4: Apply ripple formula**

$$\Delta i_p = \frac{V_s D}{L_m f_s} \implies L_m = \frac{V_s D}{\Delta i_p \cdot f_s}$$

$$L_m = \frac{100 \times 0.4}{0.167 \times 100{,}000} = \frac{40}{16{,}667} = 2.4 \text{ mH}$$

$$\boxed{L_m \geq 2.4 \text{ mH}}$$

**Step 5: Verification (using energy storage)**

$$E_{\text{stored}} = \frac{1}{2} L_m I_{p,\text{peak}}^2$$

$I_{p,\text{peak}} = I_{p,\text{avg}} + \Delta i_p/2 = 0.667 + 0.0835 = 0.75$ A

$$E_{\text{stored}} = 0.5 \times 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.75)^2 = 6.75 \times 10^{-4} \text{ J} = 675 \, \mu J$$

Energy delivered per cycle: $E_{\text{cycle}} = P_o/f_s = 25/100{,}000 = 250 \,\mu J$. Ratio ≈ 2.7× — well above the boundary, confirming CCM. ✓

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Ripple Comparison Across Topologies

| Topology | $\Delta i_L$ Formula | $\Delta V_o$ Formula | Ripple Worst-Case At |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buck | $V_o(1-D)/(Lf_s)$ | $V_o(1-D)/(8LCf_s^2)$ | $D = 0.5$ (symmetric) |
| Boost | $V_s D/(Lf_s)$ | $I_o D/(Cf_s)$ | $D \to 1$ (large) |
| Buck-Boost | $V_s D/(Lf_s)$ | $I_o D/(Cf_s)$ | $D \to 0.5$ |
| **Flyback (Isolated)** | $V_s D/(L_m f_s)$ | $I_o D/(Cf_s)$ | $D \to 0.5$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flyback Converter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The Flyback converter is best understood as a *functional architecture* of energy flow, rather than a literal schematic.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[DC Input Source Vs] -->|Primary Current| SW{MOSFET Switch S}
    SW -->|Stores Energy| CORE[Gapped Ferrite Core Coupled Inductor Lm n Np Ns]
    CORE -->|Releases Energy| D{Secondary Diode D}
    D -->|Charges| CAP[Output Filter Capacitor C]
    CAP -->|Supplies| LOAD[DC Load R]
    LOAD -->|Ground Return| SRC

    CTRL[Control Circuit PWM] -->|Drives Gate| SW
    CAP -->|Feedback Vfb| CTRL

    classDef power fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef magnetic fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class SRC,CAP power
    class CORE magnetic
    class SW,D,CTRL control
    class LOAD load
```

> **Reading the Diagram**:
> - **Orange** = Power flow path (DC energy transfer).
> - **Blue** = Magnetic energy storage (the gapped core is the *heart* of the Flyback).
> - **Purple** = Control elements (switch, diode, PWM controller).
> - **Green** = Load subsystem.

---

### 4.2 Flyback CCM Operating Waveforms

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Interval1["Interval 1: Switch ON (0 to DT)"]
        S1A[Gate Signal Vgs HIGH] --> S1B[MOSFET Closed]
        S1B --> S1C[Primary Current ip Ramps UP]
        S1C --> S1D[Secondary Diode Reverse Biased]
        S1D --> S1E[Energy Stored in Core Gap]
    end

    subgraph Interval2["Interval 2: Switch OFF DT to T"]
        S2A[Gate Signal Vgs LOW] --> S2B[MOSFET Open]
        S2B --> S2C[Polarity Reverses on Secondary]
        S2C --> S2D[Secondary Diode Forward Biased]
        S2D --> S2E[Current is Flows to Load and Capacitor]
        S2E --> S2F[Energy Released to Output]
    end

    Interval1 ==> Interval2
    Interval2 ==> Interval1
```

---

### 4.3 Ripple Generation Mechanism — Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    PULSE[Pulsed Energy from Switching] -->|Delta iL| L[Inductor Smooths Current]
    L -->|DC + AC Component| NODE[Junction Node]
    NODE -->|DC Part| R[Load R]
    NODE -->|AC Part| C[Capacitor Smooths Voltage]
    C -->|Almost DC| VOUT[Output Voltage vo with small ripple Delta Vo]
    R -->|Almost DC| VOUT

    classDef pulse fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,stroke-width:2px
    classDef smooth fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef out fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px

    class PULSE pulse
    class L,C smooth
    class R,VOUT out
```

> **Interpretation**: The inductor *absorbs* the AC component of the pulsating current and *delivers* the DC component to the load. The capacitor *absorbs* the AC component of the node voltage and *delivers* the DC output voltage. The interplay between L and C defines the *ripple magnitude*.

---

### 4.4 Flyback Equivalent Circuit Reflected to Primary

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    VS[Input Vs] --> LP[Lm Magnetizing Inductance]
    LP --> REF[Reflected Load Rprime = n squared R]
    REF --> RET[Return to Vs]
    SW2[Switch Across Lm During OFF] -.->|Captures| LP

    classDef mag fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px
    classDef elec fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px

    class LP,REF mag
    class VS,RET,SW2 elec
```

> **Key Insight**: When reflected to the primary, the Flyback looks exactly like a **buck-boost converter** with $L = L_m$ and a load $R' = n^2 R$. This is why its transfer ratio is $D/[n(1-D)]$ — the $n$ comes from the turns ratio and $(1-D)$ from the topology.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM) in a DC-DC converter. What is the role of the air gap in a Flyback transformer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **CCM (1 mark):** Continuous Conduction Mode is the operating condition of a switching converter in which the inductor (or magnetizing) current remains *strictly positive* throughout the entire switching period — i.e., the current never falls to zero.
>
> **Air Gap Role (2 marks):** In a Flyback transformer, the air gap:
> 1. **Stores magnetic energy** in the gap field, which is the *primary* energy transfer mechanism ($E = \frac{1}{2} L_m I_p^2$).
> 2. **Prevents core saturation** by lowering the effective permeability $\mu_{\text{eff}}$, allowing higher DC bias currents without saturating the ferrite.
> 3. **Linearizes the B-H characteristic**, which makes the stored energy proportional to $I_p^2$ (a design-friendly quadratic relationship).

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Compare the output voltage ripple expressions of a Boost converter and a Flyback converter operating in CCM. Why are they mathematically similar?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Boost Converter (1 mark):**
> $$\Delta V_o = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s}$$
> During switch ON, the boost diode is reverse-biased and the output capacitor must supply the entire load current $I_o$ for duration $DT_s$.
>
> **Flyback Converter (1 mark):**
> $$\Delta V_o = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s}$$
> During switch ON, the Flyback secondary diode is reverse-biased and the output capacitor again supplies the entire load current for $DT_s$.
>
> **Reason for Similarity (1 mark):** Both topologies have the property that the output capacitor is *isolated* from the input source during the switch ON-time. In both cases, the capacitor discharges into the load alone during $DT_s$, producing identical ripple expressions. The Flyback's turns ratio $n$ affects only the transfer ratio, not the ripple mechanism.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **output voltage ripple** in a Boost converter operating in CCM. Explain the role of the output capacitor in determining the ripple magnitude. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram and CCM operating waveforms, explain the operation of an **isolated Flyback converter**. Derive the voltage transfer ratio $V_o/V_s$ in terms of duty cycle $D$ and turns ratio $n$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(a)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Circuit Operation in Two Sub-Intervals**

- **Switch ON** (duration $DT_s$): The MOSFET is closed. The inductor charges linearly with slope $V_s/L$. The diode is reverse-biased, and the output capacitor *alone* supplies the load. Capacitor voltage *decreases*.
- **Switch OFF** (duration $(1-D)T_s$): The MOSFET opens. The inductor current flows through the diode into the output. The capacitor *charges* with current $(i_L - I_o)$.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Capacitor Current Waveform Analysis**

The capacitor current $i_C = i_L - I_o$ (when diode is ON) and $i_C = -I_o$ (when diode is OFF). The waveform is a piecewise linear current with average value zero (steady state).

The **charge removed from the capacitor** during the switch ON-time is:

$$Q = I_o \cdot DT_s$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Derive the Ripple Expression**

Since the capacitor voltage ripple is the change caused by removing charge $Q$:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{Q}{C} = \frac{I_o \cdot D \cdot T_s}{C} = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Role of the Capacitor**

The output capacitor *smooths* the pulsating output current. A larger $C$ → smaller $\Delta V_o$. The capacitor's ESR also contributes to ripple: $\Delta V_{o,\text{ESR}} = \text{ESR} \cdot \Delta i_C$, which often dominates in low-voltage, high-current designs.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.A(b)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Circuit Diagram and Description**

The Flyback converter consists of:
- DC source $V_s$
- MOSFET switch S in series with the primary winding $N_p$
- Coupled inductor with gapped core, secondary winding $N_s$
- Secondary-side diode D (oriented to conduct only during switch OFF)
- Output filter capacitor C and load R

**Step 2 (2 marks): Operating Principle**

- **During Switch ON ($0 \to DT_s$)**: Switch closes. Primary current $i_p$ ramps up from $I_{p,\min}$ to $I_{p,\max}$. Voltage $V_s$ is applied across primary; reflected voltage $V_s/n$ reverse-biases diode D. Energy $E = \frac{1}{2} L_m (I_{p,\max}^2 - I_{p,\min}^2)$ is stored in the air gap. Load is supplied by capacitor C.
- **During Switch OFF ($DT_s \to T_s$)**: Switch opens. Magnetizing current forces dot-end of secondary to go negative (per dot convention), forward-biasing diode D. Secondary current ramps down. Energy is transferred to C and load.

**Step 3 (2 marks): Derivation of Transfer Ratio**

Apply volt-second balance on the magnetizing inductance $L_m$:

$$V_{L,\text{on}} \cdot DT_s + V_{L,\text{off}} \cdot (1-D)T_s = 0$$

With $V_{L,\text{on}} = V_s$ and $V_{L,\text{off}} = -V_o/n$ (reflected secondary voltage):

$$V_s \cdot D - \frac{V_o}{n} \cdot (1-D) = 0$$

$$\boxed{\frac{V_o}{V_s} = \frac{nD}{1-D}}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark): Note on Polarity**

The output is *isolated* and *referenced to the secondary ground*. The output polarity depends on the diode orientation. There is **no negative sign** in the Flyback transfer ratio (unlike buck-boost) because the secondary side *flips* the reference.

---

#### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expressions for **inductor current ripple** $\Delta i_L$ and **output voltage ripple** $\Delta V_o$ in a **Buck converter operating in CCM**. Clearly state all assumptions used in the small-ripple approximation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **Flyback converter** operates with $V_s = 100$ V, $V_o = 24$ V, $P_o = 50$ W, $f_s = 80$ kHz, $D = 0.45$, and $n = 4$. Compute:
   (i) Average primary current $I_{p,\text{avg}}$,
   (ii) Primary peak current $I_{p,\text{peak}}$ given $L_m = 600$ µH,
   (iii) Output voltage ripple $\Delta V_o$ for $C = 470$ µF,
   (iv) Switch voltage stress $V_{ds,\text{max}}$. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(a)

**Step 1 (1 mark): Assumptions of Small-Ripple Approximation**

1. Switching ripples are *small* compared to DC values: $\Delta V_o \ll V_o$, $\Delta i_L \ll I_L$.
2. Transistor and diode are *ideal* (zero on-state voltage, infinite off-state impedance).
3. Inductor and capacitor are *ideal* (no series resistance, no leakage).
4. *Steady state*: average inductor voltage and capacitor current are zero over one cycle.

**Step 2 (2 marks): Inductor Current Ripple Derivation**

The voltage across the inductor:
- During ON-time ($DT_s$): $V_{L,\text{on}} = V_s - V_o$
- During OFF-time ($(1-D)T_s$): $V_{L,\text{off}} = -V_o$

Current ripple during ON-time:

$$\Delta i_L = \frac{(V_s - V_o) \cdot DT_s}{L} = \frac{V_s D(1-D)T_s}{L} = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{L f_s}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta i_L = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{L f_s}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Output Voltage Ripple Derivation**

The capacitor supplies the AC component of inductor current, which is a *symmetric triangular waveform* with peak-to-peak amplitude $\Delta i_L$. The average current into the capacitor over one cycle is zero (steady state). The peak-to-peak voltage swing is:

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta i_L}{8 f_s C}$$

Substituting $\Delta i_L$:

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o = \frac{V_o (1-D)}{8 L C f_s^2}}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Discussion and ESR Effect**

In real designs, the **ESR** of the output capacitor contributes an additional ripple:

$$\Delta V_{o,\text{total}} = \Delta V_{o,\text{ideal}} + \Delta i_L \cdot R_{\text{ESR}}$$

For low-voltage high-current outputs, the ESR contribution often *dominates*. Designers mitigate this by using ceramic + electrolytic *parallel* capacitor banks.

---

##### Model Solution for Q.B(b)

**Given Data:**
$V_s = 100$ V, $V_o = 24$ V, $P_o = 50$ W, $f_s = 80$ kHz, $D = 0.45$, $n = 4$, $L_m = 600$ µH, $C = 470$ µF.

**(i) Average Primary Current (2 marks)**

$$I_o = \frac{P_o}{V_o} = \frac{50}{24} = 2.083 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{p,\text{avg}} = \frac{I_o}{n} \cdot \frac{D}{1-D} = \frac{2.083}{4} \cdot \frac{0.45}{0.55} = 0.5208 \times 0.8182 = 0.426 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{p,\text{avg}} = 0.426 \text{ A}}$$

**(ii) Primary Peak Current (2 marks)**

Current ripple on the primary:

$$\Delta i_p = \frac{V_s D}{L_m f_s} = \frac{100 \times 0.45}{600 \times 10^{-6} \times 80{,}000} = \frac{45}{48} = 0.9375 \text{ A}$$

Peak current:

$$I_{p,\text{peak}} = I_{p,\text{avg}} + \frac{\Delta i_p}{2} = 0.426 + 0.469 = 0.895 \text{ A}$$

$$\boxed{I_{p,\text{peak}} = 0.895 \text{ A}}$$

**(iii) Output Voltage Ripple (2 marks)**

$$\Delta V_o = \frac{I_o D}{C f_s} = \frac{2.083 \times 0.45}{470 \times 10^{-6} \times 80{,}000} = \frac{0.9375}{37.6} = 0.0249 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V_o \approx 24.9 \text{ mV} \quad (0.104\% \text{ of } V_o)}$$

**(iv) Switch Voltage Stress (1 mark)**

$$V_{ds,\text{max}} = V_s + n V_o = 100 + 4 \times 24 = 100 + 96 = 196 \text{ V}$$

$$\boxed{V_{ds,\text{max}} = 196 \text{ V}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Transfer Ratio (–2 marks):** Buck-boost and Flyback outputs are referenced to a *floating* secondary ground. Do not insert a negative sign in Flyback's transfer ratio (it is the buck-boost *that has* the inversion; Flyback recovers the polarity via the secondary winding orientation).
> 2. **Forgetting $(1-D)$ in CCM average current (–2 marks):** In Flyback, the secondary diode conducts only for $(1-D)T_s$. Therefore the average secondary current is $I_o$, but the average primary current includes the $D/(1-D)$ factor.
> 3. **Ignoring ESR (–1 mark):** In capacitor ripple derivations, mention ESR contribution in real designs — examiners specifically look for this *practical insight*.
> 4. **Not specifying ripple is peak-to-peak (–1 mark):** Always state whether $\Delta V_o$ is peak-to-peak (pp) or RMS. The KTU standard convention is **peak-to-peak**.
> 5. **Switch voltage stress (–2 marks):** Students often forget the reflected secondary voltage $nV_o$ and compute only $V_s$ — this loses full marks on the stress calculation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Read This Before Walking into the Exam Hall**

- [x] **CCM Definition**: Inductor current never falls to zero; small-ripple approximation valid.
- [x] **Buck Transfer Ratio**: $V_o/V_s = D$
- [x] **Boost Transfer Ratio**: $V_o/V_s = 1/(1-D)$
- [x] **Buck-Boost Transfer Ratio**: $V_o/V_s = -D/(1-D)$ (inverted output)
- [x] **Flyback Transfer Ratio**: $V_o/V_s = D/[n(1-D)]$ (isolated, non-inverted)
- [x] **Inductor Current Ripple — Buck**: $\Delta i_L = V_o(1-D)/(Lf_s)$
- [x] **Inductor Current Ripple — Boost**: $\Delta i_L = V_s D/(Lf_s)$
- [x] **Output Voltage Ripple — Buck**: $\Delta V_o = V_o(1-D)/(8LCf_s^2)$
- [x] **Output Voltage Ripple — Boost/Flyback**: $\Delta V_o = I_o D/(Cf_s)$
- [x] **Flyback Switch Voltage Stress**: $V_{ds,\text{max}} = V_s + nV_o$
- [x] **Flyback Diode Voltage Stress**: $V_D = V_o + V_s/n$
- [x] **Flyback Average Primary Current**: $I_{p,\text{avg}} = (I_o/n) \cdot [D/(1-D)]$
- [x] **Air Gap Purpose**: Stores energy, prevents saturation, linearizes B-H curve.
- [x] **Energy Storage**: $E = \frac{1}{2} L_m I_p^2$ (stored in magnetic field of gap).
- [x] **Reflected Load**: $R' = n^2 R$ (used for primary-side analysis).
- [x] **Practical Frequency**: $f_s = 20$ kHz – 500 kHz.
- [x] **Practical Power Range of Flyback**: 0 – 150 W (above this, forward or half-bridge converters are preferred).
- [x] **Common KTU Pitfall**: Always declare *small-ripple approximation* and *ideal components* before starting any derivation.
- [x] **Real-World Insight**: Laptop chargers, mobile adapters, and TV standby SMPS are *all* Flyback converters.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Forward Converter — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Forward Converter** is a single-switch, isolated, step-down (buck-derived) DC-to-DC switching converter that uses a high-frequency transformer for galvanic isolation and voltage scaling, combined with a third "reset" winding to demagnetize the transformer core during the switch OFF-time. It belongs to the family of PWM-controlled, hard-switched, fixed-frequency DC-DC converters and operates on the principle of **unidirectional energy transfer** (unlike the flyback converter, which stores energy in the transformer magnetizing inductance during ON-time).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:**
> "A Forward Converter is a buck-derived isolated DC-DC converter in which energy is transferred directly to the load during the ON-time of the primary switch through a high-frequency transformer, with a demagnetizing (reset) winding used to return the transformer's magnetizing energy back to the input source during the OFF-time."

**Standard Nomenclature (as per KTU 2024 Module 3):**

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $V_s$ | DC Input Supply Voltage | Volts (V) |
| $V_o$ | Average Output Voltage | Volts (V) |
| $D$ | Duty Cycle (Switch ON-time fraction) | Dimensionless (0 < D < 1) |
| $N_1$ | Primary Winding Turns | Turns |
| $N_2$ | Secondary Winding Turns | Turns |
| $N_3$ | Reset (Tertiary) Winding Turns | Turns |
| $L_m$ | Magnetizing Inductance (referred to primary) | Henry (H) |
| $L_o$ | Output Filter Inductor | Henry (H) |
| $C_o$ | Output Filter Capacitor | Farad (F) |
| $R$ | Load Resistance | Ohm (Ω) |
| $f_s$ | Switching Frequency | Hertz (Hz) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **"Water Pump with a One-Way Valve & Drain Pipe" Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine a **water tank (the transformer core)** with an inlet pipe (primary winding), an outlet pipe (secondary winding), and a small **drain pipe (reset winding)**.
> - When the **switch (pump)** turns **ON**, water is forced in through the inlet. Since the outlet has a **one-way valve**, water simultaneously flows out to the consumer (load) AND a little water is stored in the tank.
> - When the switch turns **OFF**, the inlet closes, but the tank still has stored water. Without the **drain pipe (reset winding)**, the tank would overflow (saturate the core). The reset winding acts like a drain that returns this leftover water back to the source during OFF-time, **cleaning the tank** before the next cycle.
> - This direct "pass-through while the pump is ON" is what makes it a **Forward** Converter (energy moves *forward* to the load during conduction, rather than being *stored and released* as in the flyback).

## 1.3 Why Forward Converter? — Engineering Motivation

- Provides **galvanic isolation** (essential for SMPS, EV chargers, solar inverters, medical equipment).
- **Lower peak current** in the primary compared to flyback (energy transferred, not stored).
- Suitable for **medium-to-high power** applications (typically **100 W to 500 W+**).
- Better transformer utilization than flyback at higher power levels.
- Used in **telecom power supplies, laptop adapters, server SMPS, and DC motor drives**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Forward = Flow through; Flyback = Fly back to store."* Both are isolated, both use a transformer, but only forward transfers energy in real-time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Circuit Topology

A standard single-switch Forward Converter consists of:

1. **DC Input Source** ($V_s$)
2. **Power Switch** (MOSFET/IGBT) on the primary side
3. **High-Frequency Transformer** with three windings:
   - Primary ($N_1$)
   - Secondary ($N_2$)
   - Reset/Tertiary ($N_3$)
4. **Output Rectifier Diode** ($D_1$) — forward-biases during switch ON
5. **Freewheeling Diode** ($D_2$) — conducts during switch OFF
6. **LC Output Filter** ($L_o$ and $C_o$)
7. **Load** ($R$)

## 2.2 Modes of Operation (CCM — Continuous Conduction Mode)

### **Mode 1: Switch ON (Interval $DT_s$)**

- Switch is closed; primary sees $V_s$ across $N_1$.
- Magnetizing current $i_m$ ramps up linearly from $I_{m,min}$ to $I_{m,max}$.
- Voltage across secondary: $V_{sec} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$.
- Diode $D_1$ is forward-biased → conducts; $D_2$ is reverse-biased.
- Energy flows **forward** to the output filter and load.
- Voltage across output filter inductor: $V_{Lo} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} - V_o$.
- Inductor current $i_{Lo}$ ramps **up** (load is being supplied + capacitor recharged).

### **Mode 2: Switch OFF — Reset Mode (Interval $D_3 T_s$)**

- Switch opens; primary current path is broken.
- Polarities on all windings **reverse** (flyback action begins).
- Diode $D_3$ (across the primary, in series with $N_3$) becomes forward-biased.
- Voltage across primary is now **clamped** to $V_s \cdot \dfrac{N_1}{N_3}$.
- Magnetizing current $i_m$ ramps **down** from $I_{m,max}$ to zero.
- This is the **demagnetization** process — energy is returned to $V_s$ through $N_3$ and $D_3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Design Constraint:** The reset time $D_3 T_s$ must complete **before** the next switching cycle begins. This requires $D_3 \le (1 - D)$, which yields the famous **maximum duty cycle limit**.

### **Mode 3: Freewheeling (Interval $(1 - D - D_3) T_s$)**

- Once $i_m$ reaches zero, $D_3$ turns off.
- Primary is idle; $D_1$ is reverse-biased; $D_2$ conducts.
- Output inductor $L_o$ freewheels through $D_2$, supplying the load.
- $V_{Lo} = -V_o$ → inductor current ramps **down**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $V_o = V_s \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Ideal CCM output voltage (core must be fully reset) | V |
| 2 | $D_3 = D \cdot \dfrac{N_3}{N_1}$ | Reset duty cycle (fraction of switching period) | — |
| 3 | $D + D_3 \le 1$ | **Maximum duty cycle constraint** (must fully reset) | — |
| 4 | $D_{max} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_1 + N_3}$ | Maximum allowable duty cycle (when $N_3 = N_1$, $D_{max} = 0.5$) | — |
| 5 | $I_p = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot I_{Lo,avg} + I_{m,avg}$ | Primary switch peak current | A |
| 6 | $\Delta I_{Lo} = \dfrac{V_o (1-D)}{L_o f_s}$ | Output inductor ripple current | A |
| 7 | $\Delta V_o = \dfrac{\Delta I_{Lo}}{8 C_o f_s}$ | Output voltage ripple (CCM) | V |
| 8 | $I_{m,peak} = \dfrac{V_s D T_s}{L_m} = \dfrac{V_s D}{L_m f_s}$ | Peak magnetizing current | A |
| 9 | $V_{DS,peak} = V_s \left(1 + \dfrac{N_1}{N_3}\right)$ | Peak voltage stress on primary switch | V |
| 10 | $V_{sec} = V_s \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1}$ | Secondary voltage during ON | V |
| 11 | $\text{Turns ratio } a = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Effective turns ratio | — |
| 12 | $P_o = \dfrac{V_o^2}{R}$ | Output power (resistive load) | W |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Design Choice:** For a symmetrical design where reset winding equals primary ($N_3 = N_1$), $D_{max} = 0.5$. This is the most common KTU-exam design and **should be memorized**.

## 2.4 Why This Matters in Real Engineering

- **Telecom 48 V SMPS:** Forward converter steps down 48 V → 12 V or 5 V with isolation.
- **EV Auxiliary Power Module (APM):** High-voltage battery (400 V) → 12 V for accessories.
- **Solar Microinverters:** DC-DC stage before DC-AC inversion.
- **Medical & Aerospace:** Isolation barrier required by safety standards (IEC 60601, DO-160).
- **Server PSUs (80+ Titanium):** High-efficiency forward/fwd-resonant topologies dominate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage $V_o$ in CCM (Volt-Second Balance on $L_o$)

### Step 1: Identify the two states of the output filter inductor $L_o$

During **switch ON** (duration $D T_s$):

$$
V_{Lo,ON} = V_s \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} - V_o
$$

During **switch OFF + freewheeling** (duration $(1 - D) T_s$):

$$
V_{Lo,OFF} = -V_o
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Inductor Volt-Second Balance (steady-state)

In steady-state, the average voltage across an inductor over one switching period must equal zero (otherwise the current would drift unboundedly).

$$
\int_0^{T_s} V_{Lo}(t) \, dt = 0
$$

$$
\left(V_s \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} - V_o\right) \cdot D T_s + (-V_o) \cdot (1 - D) T_s = 0
$$

### Step 3: Solve for $V_o$

Divide both sides by $T_s$:

$$
\left(V_s \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} - V_o\right) D - V_o(1 - D) = 0
$$

Expand:

$$
V_s \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot D - V_o D - V_o + V_o D = 0
$$

The $-V_o D$ and $+V_o D$ cancel:

$$
V_s \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot D - V_o = 0
$$

$$
\boxed{V_o = V_s \cdot D \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** This is identical in form to a non-isolated buck converter, except scaled by the turns ratio $N_2/N_1$. The transformer's turns ratio gives **design freedom** to optimize $V_o$ for any $V_s$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reset Duty Cycle $D_3$

### Step 1: Voltage across primary during ON

$$
V_{L1,ON} = V_s
$$

### Step 2: Voltage across primary during reset (OFF)

When $D_3$ conducts, the reset winding $N_3$ has voltage $V_s$ across it (the diode $D_3$ connects the free end of $N_3$ back to $V_s$). Hence the primary sees:

$$
V_{L1,OFF} = -V_s \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_3}
$$

### Step 3: Apply volt-second balance on $L_m$

$$
V_s \cdot D T_s - V_s \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_3} \cdot D_3 T_s = 0
$$

### Step 4: Solve

$$
D \cdot V_s = D_3 \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_3}
$$

$$
\boxed{D_3 = D \cdot \frac{N_3}{N_1}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Maximum Duty Cycle

For the core to **fully reset before the next cycle**:

$$
D + D_3 \le 1
$$

Substitute $D_3$:

$$
D + D \cdot \frac{N_3}{N_1} \le 1
$$

$$
D \left(1 + \frac{N_3}{N_1}\right) \le 1
$$

$$
\boxed{D \le \frac{N_1}{N_1 + N_3}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For the **most common KTU design case** $N_3 = N_1$:
> $D_{max} = 0.5$

## 3.4 Derivation: Peak Voltage Stress on Switch

When the switch is OFF, the full reflected reset voltage appears in series with $V_s$ across the switch drain-source:

$$
V_{DS,peak} = V_s + V_s \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_3}
$$

$$
\boxed{V_{DS,peak} = V_s \left(1 + \frac{N_1}{N_3}\right)}
$$

For $N_3 = N_1$: $V_{DS,peak} = 2 V_s$. This is why forward converter MOSFETs are typically rated at **2× the input voltage**.

## 3.5 Complete Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem)

> **Given:** $V_s = 48$ V, $N_1 : N_2 : N_3 = 10 : 1 : 10$, $L_o = 200\ \mu H$, $C_o = 100\ \mu F$, $R = 5\ \Omega$, $f_s = 100$ kHz, $D = 0.4$.

**Step 1: Output voltage**

$$
V_o = 48 \cdot 0.4 \cdot \frac{1}{10} = 1.92\ \text{V}
$$

**Step 2: Reset duty cycle**

$$
D_3 = 0.4 \cdot \frac{10}{10} = 0.4
$$

**Step 3: Check $D + D_3 \le 1$**

$$
0.4 + 0.4 = 0.8 \le 1\ \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Output current**

$$
I_o = \frac{V_o}{R} = \frac{1.92}{5} = 0.384\ \text{A}
$$

**Step 5: Inductor ripple**

$$
\Delta I_{Lo} = \frac{1.92 \cdot (1 - 0.4)}{200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{1.152}{20} = 0.0576\ \text{A} = 57.6\ \text{mA}
$$

**Step 6: Output voltage ripple**

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{0.0576}{8 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{0.0576}{80} = 0.72\ \text{mV}
$$

**Step 7: Peak switch voltage**

$$
V_{DS,peak} = 48 \left(1 + \frac{10}{10}\right) = 96\ \text{V}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Check:** All numbers must come out realistic. A switch rated for **≥ 150 V** would be selected here for safety margin.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forward Converter — Complete Circuit Schematic

```mermaid
graph TB
    VS[DC Source Vs]:::input
    SW[Power Switch MOSFET]:::switch
    T1[Transformer Primary N1]:::xfmr
    T2[Transformer Secondary N2]:::xfmr
    T3[Reset Winding N3]:::xfmr

    CO[Output Capacitor Co]:::filter

    VS --- SW
    SW --- T1
    T1 === T2
    T1 === T3

    CO --- VS
    T3 --- D3
    D3 --- VS

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input DC Source Vs] --> B[PWM Gate Driver]
    B --> C[Power Switch]
    C --> D[HF Transformer with Reset Winding]
    D --> E[Secondary Rectifier D1]
    D --> F[Reset Clamp D3 and N3]
    E --> G[LC Output Filter Lo Co]
    G --> H[Load R]
    F --> A
    G --> I[Feedback to PWM Controller]
    I --> B
```

## 4.3 Modes of Operation — State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Mode1_ON: Switch Closes
    Mode1_ON: Mode 1 — Switch ON\nEnergy Transfer to Load\nLm charges, Lo charges
    Mode1_ON --> Mode2_Reset: Switch Opens
    Mode2_Reset: Mode 2 — Reset\nCore Demagnetizing\nD3 conducts, energy to Vs
    Mode2_Reset --> Mode3_Freewheel: Lm current reaches zero
    Mode3_Freewheel: Mode 3 — Freewheel\nD2 conducts\nLo supplies load
    Mode3_Freewheel --> Mode1_ON: Next switching cycle
```

## 4.4 Key Waveforms (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    W1[Vgs - Gate Signal PWM]:::wave
    W2[Primary Voltage Vpri]:::wave
    W3[Magnetizing Current Im]:::wave
    W4[Secondary Voltage Vsec]:::wave
    W5[Output Inductor Current ILo]:::wave
    W6[Output Voltage Vo]:::wave

    W1 -->|trigger| W2
    W2 -->|induced| W3
    W3 -->|reflected| W4
    W4 -->|rectified| W5
    W5 -->|filtered| W6

    classDef wave fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000
```
