<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**
**What is a forward converter? Why is a demagnetizing (reset) winding required in it?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **forward converter** is an isolated, buck-derived DC-DC converter that transfers energy from input to load through a high-frequency transformer **during the ON-time of the primary switch** (3 marks: 1 for isolated, 1 for buck-derived, 1 for energy transfer during ON).

The **reset winding is required** to demagnetize the transformer core. During the ON-time, magnetizing current builds up in $L_m$. When the switch turns OFF, the magnetizing energy must be returned to the source; otherwise the core will saturate, causing switch failure. The reset winding, with its clamp diode, provides a path for this stored energy to flow back to $V_s$ during the OFF-time, resetting the flux in the core.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**State the maximum duty cycle limitation of a forward converter with a reset winding and explain why it exists.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

$$
D_{max} = \frac{N_1}{N_1 + N_3}
$$

**Reason (2 marks):** The reset winding takes a finite time $D_3 T_s$ to fully demagnetize the core. Since the total period is fixed at $T_s$, the constraint $D + D_3 \le 1$ must hold. If $D$ exceeds this limit, the next switching cycle begins **before** the core is fully reset, leading to **flux walking** and eventual **core saturation** → catastrophic switch failure.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the **operation of a single-switch forward converter** in CCM. Discuss all three modes of operation. (7 marks)

**(b)** A forward converter has $V_s = 100$ V, $N_1 : N_2 : N_3 = 5 : 1 : 5$, $D = 0.4$, $L_o = 250\ \mu H$, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $R = 10\ \Omega$. Calculate: (i) $V_o$, (ii) reset duty cycle, (iii) peak switch voltage, (iv) output voltage ripple. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Operation (7 marks):**

Refer to the circuit schematic in Section 4.1. Discuss:

- **Mode 1 (Switch ON, $0 \le t \le DT_s$):** Primary sees $V_s$; $D_1$ conducts; $D_2$ blocks; $L_m$ charges linearly; $L_o$ charges (1.5 marks).
- **Mode 2 (Reset, $DT_s \le t \le (D+D_3)T_s$):** Switch OFF; $D_3$ conducts; energy in $L_m$ returns to source via $N_3$; flux resets (1.5 marks).
- **Mode 3 (Freewheel, $(D+D_3)T_s \le t \le T_s$):** $D_3$ stops; $D_2$ conducts; $L_o$ freewheels; load supplied by $L_o$ and $C_o$ (1 mark).
- **Waveforms:** Describe $V_{DS}$, $i_m$, $i_{Lo}$ qualitatively (1 mark).
- **Reset duty cycle equation** derivation (2 marks).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V_s = 100$ V, $N_1 = 5$, $N_2 = 1$, $N_3 = 5$, $D = 0.4$, $L_o = 250\ \mu H$, $f_s = 50$ kHz, $R = 10\ \Omega$.

**(i) Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_o = V_s \cdot D \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 100 \cdot 0.4 \cdot \frac{1}{5} = 8\ \text{V}
$$

**(ii) Reset Duty Cycle (1 mark):**

$$
D_3 = D \cdot \frac{N_3}{N_1} = 0.4 \cdot \frac{5}{5} = 0.4
$$

Check: $D + D_3 = 0.8 \le 1\ \checkmark$

**(iii) Peak Switch Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{DS,peak} = V_s \left(1 + \frac{N_1}{N_3}\right) = 100 \cdot \left(1 + \frac{5}{5}\right) = 200\ \text{V}
$$

**(iv) Output Voltage Ripple (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta I_{Lo} = \frac{V_o(1-D)}{L_o f_s} = \frac{8 \cdot 0.6}{250 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{4.8}{12.5} = 0.384\ \text{A}
$$

Assume $C_o = 100\ \mu F$ (standard; if not given, state assumption):

$$
\Delta V_o = \frac{\Delta I_{Lo}}{8 C_o f_s} = \frac{0.384}{8 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50 \times 10^3} = \frac{0.384}{40} = 9.6\ \text{mV}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **output voltage of a forward converter** in CCM, clearly stating the volt-second balance principle. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the **forward converter with the flyback converter** based on: (i) energy transfer mechanism, (ii) transformer utilization, (iii) typical power range, (iv) output ripple, and (v) clamping requirement. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. State volt-second balance principle: in steady state, $\int_0^{T_s} V_{Lo} \, dt = 0$ (1 mark).
2. Identify $V_{Lo,ON} = V_s (N_2/N_1) - V_o$ and $V_{Lo,OFF} = -V_o$ (2 marks).
3. Write integral equation with durations $DT_s$ and $(1-D)T_s$ (2 marks).
4. Solve step-by-step to obtain $V_o = V_s \cdot D \cdot (N_2/N_1)$ (2 marks).

> Refer to Section 3.1 for full algebraic steps.

**(b) Comparison Table (7 marks = 1.4 marks each):**

| Parameter | Forward Converter | Flyback Converter |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| (i) Energy transfer | Direct (passes through transformer) | Indirect (stored in $L_m$, released later) |
| (ii) Transformer utilization | High (no air gap needed, $L_m$ is parasitic) | Low (requires large air gap; core stores energy) |
| (iii) Power range | 100 W – 500 W+ (medium-high) | 0 – 150 W (low power) |
| (iv) Output ripple | Low (continuous $L_o$ current) | Higher (discontinuous pulses) |
| (v) Clamping | **Reset winding required** to demagnetize core | Inherent (stored energy delivered to load) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the turns ratio** in $V_o$ equation: Students often write $V_o = V_s D$ (buck) and lose 2 marks. Always include $N_2/N_1$ for the forward converter.
> 2. **Not checking $D + D_3 \le 1$**: Examiners allocate marks for verifying the design constraint. Skipping it = 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Confusing Forward and Flyback**: A frequent mistake. Forward = energy transfer during ON; Flyback = energy storage during ON. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 4. **Switch voltage stress**: Many students compute only $V_s$ and forget the reflected reset voltage. Correct: $V_{DS,peak} = V_s(1 + N_1/N_3)$.
> 5. **Wrong ripple formula**: $\Delta V_o = \Delta I_{Lo} / (8 C_o f_s)$ is the CCM approximation; using $1/(2 C_o f_s)$ or forgetting the factor of 8 is a common error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Summary — Read Before Exam:**

- [x] **Forward Converter** = isolated, buck-derived, single-switch, uses HF transformer with **reset winding**.
- [x] Energy flows **forward to load during switch ON** (key distinction from flyback).
- [x] **Core equation:** $V_o = V_s \cdot D \cdot (N_2/N_1)$.
- [x] **Reset duty cycle:** $D_3 = D \cdot (N_3/N_1)$.
- [x] **Critical limit:** $D + D_3 \le 1$ (must fully reset before next cycle).
- [x] **Standard design** ($N_3 = N_1$) → $D_{max} = 0.5$.
- [x] **Switch voltage stress:** $V_{DS,peak} = V_s(1 + N_1/N_3)$.
- [x] **For $N_3 = N_1$:** switch sees $2 V_s$ → choose MOSFET rated at $\ge 2 V_s$ (with margin).
- [x] **Three modes:** ON (transfer), Reset (demag), Freewheel (idle).
- [x] **Output filter:** LC low-pass → continuous load current in CCM.
- [x] **Applications:** SMPS, EV APMs, telecom 48 V, server PSUs, medical isolation.
- [x] **Advantages over flyback:** Lower primary peak current, better transformer utilization at medium power.
- [x] **Disadvantages:** Requires third (reset) winding → more complex magnetics, switch voltage stress doubles.
- [x] **Design thumb rule:** Reset winding turns = primary turns (symmetric clamp).
- [x] **Memory equation block:** $V_o, D_3, D_{max}, V_{DS,peak}$ — these four equations appear in **every KTU exam**.
- [x] **Derivations to practice:** Output voltage (volt-second), reset duty cycle, max duty cycle.
- [x] **Common KTU trap:** Confusing "forward" energy transfer with "flyback" storage. Remember the analogy of the **water pump with drain pipe** (Section 1.2).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Push Pull
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEVT613 - POWER ELECTRONICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Push Pull # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Push-Pull Inverter** is a single-phase, voltage-source DC–AC converter topology that employs **two actively controlled switching devices** (BJTs / Power MOSFETs / IGBTs) connected in a symmetrical fashion to the **two halves of a center-tapped transformer primary winding**. The DC source is connected between the **center tap** of the primary and the **common return point** of the two switches. The two switches are operated in a **complementary, 180° phase-shifted** manner, each conducting for a half-cycle, thereby forcing alternating magnetic flux in the transformer core and producing a **square-wave AC voltage** at the secondary (load) terminals. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board-Examiner Definition (verbatim-ready):** > *"A push-pull inverter is a square-wave VSI that uses two switches and a center-tapped transformer to convert a DC input into a bipolar square-wave AC output, where each switch connects one half of the transformer primary to the supply for half the switching period."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Engineering Intuition Imagine a **see-saw (teeter-totter) with a pivot in the middle** — this is exactly how a push-pull inverter works magnetically. - The **DC battery** is the "pivot supply" at the center of the see-saw. - The **two switches** are two children sitting on either end — when one child "pushes down," the other end "pulls up." - The **transformer core** is the see-saw plank itself, and the **secondary winding** is a person watching from below who sees the plank oscillating up and down → producing **AC** from a **DC** input. In even simpler terms: two switches **"push"** and **"pull"** current through opposite halves of the transformer in alternating fashion — hence the name **"Push-Pull."** > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight for Exams:** A Push-Pull topology is essentially a "single-supply, common-emitter, transformer-coupled" version of the half-bridge — but with the distinct feature of **flux reversals** in a single magnetic core, allowing true bipolar AC output *without* a split DC bus. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Nomenclature (KTU Convention) | Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value / Unit | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | $V_{dc}$ | DC input / source voltage | **Volts (V)** | | $N_1$ | Total primary turns (center-tap = $N_1/2 + N_1/2$) | dimensionless | | $N_2$ | Secondary turns | dimensionless | | $n = N_2/N_1$ | Total turns ratio | dimensionless | | $f_o$ | Fundamental output frequency | **Hz** (typically **50 Hz** or **60 Hz**) | | $T_s$ | Switching period = $1/f_o$ | seconds | | $V_{sw,peak}$ | Peak blocking voltage across each switch | $= 2 V_{dc}$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **two non-negotiable design constants** every KTU student must commit to memory: > 1. Switch voltage stress = $2 V_{dc}$ > 2. Output is a **square wave** of amplitude $\pm n V_{dc}$ # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step The Push-Pull Inverter operates on the principle of **forced commutation** and **bipolar flux excitation** of a single magnetic core. The detailed logic is presented below in five sequential steps: ### Step 1 — Circuit Resting State Both switches $S_1$ and $S_2$ are OFF. No current flows in the primary. Flux in the core is zero. The output voltage $v_o(t) = 0$. ### Step 2 — Positive Half-Cycle (Switch $S_1$ ON) - Gate pulse is applied to $S_1$ for the interval $0 \leq t \leq T_s/2$. - Current path: $+V_{dc} \to S_1 \to$ **upper half** of primary $\to$ center tap $\to -V_{dc}$. - Voltage across the **upper** half of primary = $+V_{dc}$. - By **transformer action (Lenz's law)**, the **lower** half develops an induced voltage of $-V_{dc}$ (dot convention). - **Total** primary voltage from top terminal to bottom terminal = $V_{dc}$. - Secondary voltage = $+n \cdot V_{dc}$ (polarity set by dot convention). - Output across load = $+n V_{dc}$. ### Step 3 — Commutation (Both Switches OFF briefly) A small **dead-time** (typically 1–5 % of $T_s$) is provided to prevent shoot-through. During this interval, load current freewheels through anti-parallel diodes (or the load's natural inductance) and the output returns to zero. > [!NOTE] > Many KTU textbook problems **ignore the dead-time** and assume ideal complementary switching. Students must clearly state this assumption in derivations. ### Step 4 — Negative Half-Cycle (Switch $S_2$ ON) - Gate pulse is applied to $S_2$ for the interval $T_s/2 \leq t \leq T_s$. - Current path reverses: $+V_{dc} \to$ **lower half** of primary $\to$ center tap $\to S_2 \to -V_{dc}$. - Voltage across lower half = $+V_{dc}$; upper half = $-V_{dc}$. - Total primary voltage = $-V_{dc}$. - Secondary voltage = $-n \cdot V_{dc}$. - Output across load = $-n V_{dc}$. ### Step 5 — Periodic Repetition The cycle repeats with period $T_s = 1/f_o$, producing a **bipolar square wave** of amplitude $\pm n V_{dc}$ at the load. ## 2.2 Switch Voltage Stress — The "How" Behind $2V_{dc}$ When $S_1$ is ON: - Upper half of primary is at $+V_{dc}$ (relative to center tap). - Lower half is induced at $-V_{dc}$ (relative to center tap). - Therefore, the **bottom terminal of the primary is at $-V_{dc}$** with respect to the center tap. - Switch $S_2$ has its emitter at the negative rail (0 V reference) and its collector at the bottom primary terminal. - Voltage across $S_2$ = $0 - (-V_{dc}) = +V_{dc}$ (from the source side) **PLUS** the reflected voltage from the upper half. - **Net voltage across $S_2$ when OFF = $2 V_{dc}$**. This is the **single most important** and **most-asked** derivation in KTU exams on this topic. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield) | # | Quantity | Formula | Conditions / Units | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | Peak secondary voltage | $V_{o,peak} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_{dc}$ | Volts | | 2 | RMS output voltage (square wave) | $V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_{dc}$ | Volts | | 3 | Peak switch voltage | $V_{sw,peak} = 2 V_{dc}$ | Volts | | 4 | Average switch current | $I_{sw,avg} = \dfrac{I_{o,avg}}{2}$ | Amperes | | 5 | RMS switch current (square) | $I_{sw,rms} = \dfrac{I_{o,rms}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Amperes | | 6 | Output frequency | $f_o = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | Hz | | 7 | Fourier fundamental peak | $V_{o1,peak} = \dfrac{4}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_{dc}$ | Volts | | 8 | Fourier fundamental RMS | $V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_{dc}$ | Volts | | 9 | Total Harmonic Distortion (THD) | $\text{THD} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi^2}{8} - 1} \approx 48.3\,\%$ | Square wave | | 10 | Transformer volt-ampere rating | $S_T = V_{o,rms} \cdot I_{o,rms}$ | VA | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Where you will find Push-Pull Inverters in industry:** > - **Uninterruptible Power Supplies (UPS)** — low-to-medium power (≤ 5 kVA) online and line-interactive systems. > - **Solar PV micro-inverters** — module-level AC generation (200 W – 600 W). > - **Induction heating** — high-frequency (20–100 kHz) versions using MOSFETs. > - **Class-B audio amplifiers** — the audio-frequency ancestor of this topology. > - **Traction auxiliary supplies** — railway signaling and coach lighting inverters. > - **Aerospace** — 400 Hz aircraft AC bus generation. The topology is **favoured where galvanic isolation is mandatory** and where **simple gate-drive (common-emitter/source)** is desirable. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation #1 — RMS Output Voltage of a Square-Wave Push-Pull Inverter **Assumption Statement (must be written in KTU answer scripts):** Assume ideal switches, an ideal lossless transformer with turns ratio $n = N_2/N_1$, no dead-time, and a purely resistive load $R_L$. **Step 1 — Establish the output waveform equation** The secondary voltage is a periodic square wave alternating between $+n V_{dc}$ and $-n V_{dc}$ with period $T_s$. By symmetry, we analyse the interval $0 \leq t \leq T_s/2$: $$ v_o(t) = \begin{cases} +n V_{dc}, & 0 \leq t \leq \dfrac{T_s}{2} \\ -n V_{dc}, & \dfrac{T_s}{2} \leq t \leq T_s \end{cases} $$ **Step 2 — Apply the standard RMS definition** The RMS value of any periodic signal over one full period is defined as: $$ V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T_s}\int_{0}^{T_s} v_o^{2}(t)\, dt} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the piecewise expression** Because $v_o^{2}(t) = (n V_{dc})^{2}$ for **both** half-cycles (the square of $\pm nV_{dc}$ is the same positive value), the integral simplifies: $$ V_{o,rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T_s}\int_{0}^{T_s/2} (n V_{dc})^{2}\, dt + \dfrac{1}{T_s}\int_{T_s/2}^{T_s} (n V_{dc})^{2}\, dt} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals** $$ \begin{aligned} V_{o,rms} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{(n V_{dc})^{2}}{T_s} \cdot \left[ \int_{0}^{T_s/2} dt + \int_{T_s/2}^{T_s} dt \right]} \\[6pt] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{(n V_{dc})^{2}}{T_s} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{T_s}{2} + \dfrac{T_s}{2} \right]} \\[6pt] &= \sqrt{\dfrac{(n V_{dc})^{2}}{T_s} \cdot T_s} \\[6pt] &= \sqrt{(n V_{dc})^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5 — Take the positive root (RMS is always non-negative)** $$ \boxed{\,V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_{dc}\,} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** Unlike sinusoidal systems, the RMS value of a symmetric square wave equals its peak value — this is a direct consequence of the constant magnitude across the full period. --- ## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Peak Switch Blocking Voltage ($2V_{dc}$) **Setup:** Refer to the circuit. Let the bottom rail be the reference (0 V). The center tap is at $+V_{dc}$. **Step 1 — When $S_1$ is ON (analyse OFF switch $S_2$)** Current flows through the upper half. By transformer action, the lower half has an induced voltage of $-V_{dc}$ with respect to the center tap: $$ V_{\text{bottom terminal}} = V_{\text{center tap}} + (-V_{dc}) = V_{dc} - V_{dc} = 0\,\text{V (relative to bottom rail)} $$ Wait — let us redo this carefully using the centre tap as the reference, which simplifies the analysis. **Step 1 (Corrected) — Define the center tap as the reference node (0 V)** When $S_1$ is ON: - Top of primary is at $-V_{dc}$ (current enters the center tap from $+V_{dc}$ rail and exits at the top, so the top is at lower potential). Actually, the cleanest approach uses the **dot convention**. Assume the dots are at the top of both primary halves and the top of the secondary. - Voltage at center tap = $+V_{dc}$ (connected to +ve rail of supply). - Voltage at top of primary (with $S_1$ ON, $S_1$ acts as a closed switch to the negative rail) = $0$ V. - Hence, voltage across the upper half (top to center tap) = $0 - V_{dc} = -V_{dc}$. - By Lenz's law, the lower half develops $+V_{dc}$ (bottom of primary is at $2V_{dc}$ with respect to center tap). **Step 2 — Voltage across the OFF switch $S_2$** The OFF switch $S_2$ has: - Its collector at the bottom of the primary = $+2V_{dc}$ (relative to center tap). - Its emitter at the negative rail = $0$ V (relative to the same reference, since the negative rail and center tap differ by $V_{dc}$... let us use the negative rail as absolute ground). **Step 3 — Use the negative rail as absolute ground (0 V)** Then: - Center tap is at $+V_{dc}$. - Top of primary (via closed $S_1$ to ground) is at $0$ V. - Voltage across upper half = $0 - V_{dc} = -V_{dc}$. - Induced voltage on lower half = $+V_{dc}$ (by Lenz's law / transformer ratio). - Voltage at the bottom of primary = $V_{dc} + V_{dc} = +2V_{dc}$. - This is also the collector voltage of $S_2$ (since $S_2$ is between bottom-of-primary and ground). - Emitter of $S_2$ is at $0$ V (ground). $$ V_{S_2} = V_{\text{collector}} - V_{\text{emitter}} = 2V_{dc} - 0 = 2V_{dc} $$ **Final Result:** $$ \boxed{\,V_{sw,peak} = 2 V_{dc}\,} $$ **Symmetry Check:** By identical reasoning, when $S_2$ is ON, switch $S_1$ blocks $2V_{dc}$. --- ## 3.3 Derivation #3 — Fourier Series of the Output Voltage (For Harmonic Analysis) **Step 1 — Recognize the waveform as an odd, half-wave symmetric square wave** The waveform $v_o(t)$ is an odd function (anti-symmetric about the origin) and has half-wave symmetry ($v_o(t + T_s/2) = -v_o(t)$). Therefore, the Fourier series contains **only odd harmonics of the sine term**: $$ v_o(t) = \sum_{k=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} b_k \sin(k \omega t) $$ where $\omega = 2\pi f_o = 2\pi/T_s$. **Step 2 — Compute the Fourier coefficient $b_k$** $$ b_k = \dfrac{2}{T_s}\int_{0}^{T_s} v_o(t) \sin(k\omega t)\, dt $$ Splitting into the two half-cycles: $$ b_k = \dfrac{2}{T_s}\left[ \int_{0}^{T_s/2} (nV_{dc}) \sin(k\omega t)\, dt + \int_{T_s/2}^{T_s} (-nV_{dc}) \sin(k\omega t)\, dt \right] $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the first integral** $$ \begin{aligned} I_1 &= \int_{0}^{T_s/2} \sin(k\omega t)\, dt = \left[ -\dfrac{\cos(k\omega t)}{k\omega} \right]_{0}^{T_s/2} \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{1}{k\omega}\left[ 1 - \cos\!\left(\dfrac{k\pi \cdot 2\pi}{2 \cdot 2\pi}\right) \right] = \dfrac{1}{k\omega}\left[ 1 - \cos(k\pi) \right] \end{aligned} $$ Since $\cos(k\pi) = (-1)^k$ and $k$ is **odd** (because even harmonics vanish due to half-wave symmetry), $\cos(k\pi) = -1$: $$ I_1 = \dfrac{1 - (-1)}{k\omega} = \dfrac{2}{k\omega} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the second integral by symmetry** The integral from $T_s/2$ to $T_s$ of $\sin(k\omega t)$ for odd $k$ gives: $$ I_2 = -\dfrac{2}{k\omega} $$ **Step 5 — Combine the two** $$ \begin{aligned} b_k &= \dfrac{2}{T_s} \cdot nV_{dc} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{2}{k\omega} - \dfrac{2}{k\omega} \right] \\[6pt] &= \dfrac{2 nV_{dc}}{T_s k\omega} \cdot \left[ 2 + 2 \right] = \dfrac{8 nV_{dc}}{T_s k \cdot 2\pi f_o} \end{aligned} $$ Since $T_s f_o = 1$, this simplifies to: $$ b_k = \dfrac{4 nV_{dc}}{k\pi} $$ **Step 6 — Write the final Fourier series** $$ \boxed{\,v_o(t) = \dfrac{4 nV_{dc}}{\pi} \sum_{k=1,3,5,\dots}^{\infty} \dfrac{\sin(k\omega t)}{k}\,} $$ **Engineering Insight:** The fundamental ($k=1$) has amplitude $4 nV_{dc}/\pi \approx 1.273\, nV_{dc}$, which is **27.3 % higher** than the square-wave's nominal peak — a counter-intuitive result that KTU examiners love to test. --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Style) **Problem:** A push-pull inverter has $V_{dc} = 48$ V, turns ratio $N_2/N_1 = 5$, and operates at $f_o = 50$ Hz into a resistive load $R_L = 10\,\Omega$. Calculate: (a) RMS output voltage, (b) Peak switch voltage, (c) RMS fundamental component, (d) Output power. **Solution:** (a) Using the derived formula: $$ V_{o,rms} = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} \cdot V_{dc} = 5 \times 48 = 240\,\text{V} $$ (b) Switch stress: $$ V_{sw,peak} = 2 V_{dc} = 2 \times 48 = 96\,\text{V} $$ (c) Fundamental RMS: $$ V_{o1,rms} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \cdot n \cdot V_{dc} = \dfrac{2\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \times 5 \times 48 $$ $$ V_{o1,rms} = 0.9003 \times 240 \approx 216.08\,\text{V} $$ (d) Output power (using the total square-wave RMS into a resistive load): $$ P_o = \dfrac{V_{o,rms}^{2}}{R_L} = \dfrac{240^{2}}{10} = 5760\,\text{W} = 5.76\,\text{kW} $$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Power Flow Architecture of Push-Pull Inverter > [!NOTE] > The diagram below is a **functional architecture flow** rather than a literal circuit drawing — this is the KTU-recommended representation when a full schematic is impractical in text form. ```mermaid graph TB DCBLOCK["DC SOURCE V_dc
